topics.title: Write a Python program to sort a list of tuples based on the second element in each tuple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Python program to sort a list of tuples based on the second element in each tuple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2 — Sorting a List of Tuples by the Second Element

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> **Sorting a list of tuples by the second element** is a fundamental data manipulation operation in Python where a heterogeneous, index-based collection of immutable sequences is rearranged in either **ascending** or **descending** order, using a designated positional index (the second element, i.e., `tuple[1]`) as the **sort key** (also called the *key function* parameter).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment tests two core **Course Outcomes**:

- **CO1:** *Apply Python control structures, data structures, and built-in functions to solve computational problems.*
- **CO2:** *Demonstrate proficiency in using Python's functional programming constructs like `lambda`, `sorted()`, and the `key` argument.*

The Pythonic idiom used is:

```python
sorted(list_of_tuples, key=lambda x: x[1])
```

This single line accesses the second element (`x[1]`) of every tuple and uses it as the comparator basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Term:** The argument passed to the `key` parameter is called the **"key function"** (or *selector function*). It is invoked **exactly once per record** during sorting, making it highly efficient.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a school register where each row is a sealed envelope containing two items:

| Envelope (Tuple) | Item 1 (Position 0) | Item 2 (Position 1) |
|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Envelope A     | "Rahul"             | 85                  |
| Envelope B     | "Anjali"            | 92                  |
| Envelope C     | "Vikram"            | 78                  |

A teacher wants to **rank students by their marks** (Item 2), not by their name. However, the envelope is sealed — you can't tear it open and replace the items. What you *can* do is **peel back a window** to peek at the second item (the marks) and use that peek to rearrange the envelopes on a shelf.

That "window-peek" is exactly what the `key=lambda x: x[1]` does — it tells Python: *"Don't compare the whole tuple; just look at the second item when deciding the order."*

### 🔑 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Tuple:** An **immutable**, ordered, **heterogeneous** sequence in Python, defined using parentheses `()`.
- **List:** A **mutable**, ordered collection defined using square brackets `[]`.
- **Lambda Function:** An **anonymous, single-expression** function created via the `lambda` keyword — has no `def` block and no name.
- **Key Parameter:** The optional `key=` argument in `sorted()` / `list.sort()` that extracts a comparison value from each element.
- **Stable Sort:** Python's sorting algorithm (Timsort) preserves the **relative order** of equal-key elements — a property KTU examiners frequently test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric / constant used:** Time complexity of Python's built-in `sorted()` is **O(n log n)** in the worst case, courtesy of the **Timsort** hybrid algorithm (a merge + insertion sort hybrid).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Operational Mechanics — How Sorting by Second Element Works

The sorting process is broken down into four logical phases:

### Phase 1 — Input Acquisition
A list of tuples is constructed. Each tuple has **at least two elements** (we need an index `[1]` to exist).

```python
data = [('apple', 5), ('banana', 2), ('cherry', 8), ('date', 1)]
```

### Phase 2 — Key Function Definition
A `lambda` function is defined that, when given a tuple, returns only its **second element**:

```python
key_fn = lambda item: item[1]
```

The lambda is **stateless** — it does not modify the tuple (impossible anyway since tuples are immutable).

### Phase 3 — Sort Invocation
The key function is handed off to Python's internal sorting engine:

```python
result = sorted(data, key=key_fn)
```

Internally, Python's **Timsort** algorithm:
1. Computes the key for every element **once** (cached for efficiency).
2. Sorts the *list of keys*, not the list of tuples.
3. Reorders the original tuples according to the sorted keys.
4. Preserves original order for ties (stability guarantee).

### Phase 4 — Output
The sorted list is returned (or modified in-place if `.sort()` is used).

## 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Description | Return Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sorted()` function | `sorted(iterable, key=fn, reverse=False)` | Returns a **new** sorted list | `list` |
| `list.sort()` method | `list.sort(key=fn, reverse=False)` | Sorts the list **in-place** | `None` |
| Lambda for 2nd element | `lambda x: x[1]` | Selector for index 1 | Callable |
| Index access | `tuple[i]` | Zero-based positional access | Element type |
| Ascending default | `reverse=False` | Lower keys come first | — |
| Descending option | `reverse=True` | Higher keys come first | — |
| Time complexity | **T(n) = O(n log n)** | Timsort worst-case | — |
| Space complexity | **O(n)** for `sorted()`, **O(1)** auxiliary for `.sort()` | Memory profile | — |
| Stability | **True** (always) | Equal keys retain input order | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** `list.sort()` returns `None`. A common bug is `data = data.sort(key=...)` which sets `data` to `None`. Always use `sorted()` if you need a new list.

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

This pattern is **ubiquitous** in:

- **Database systems** — Sorting records by a specific column (e.g., sort employees by salary).
- **Data analytics** — Pandas DataFrames internally use key functions for `sort_values()`.
- **Computer graphics** — Z-ordering sprites by depth coordinate.
- **Networking** — Sorting packets by timestamp or priority queue.
- **Machine learning** — Sorting predictions by confidence score.
- **Web development** — Ordering search results by relevance, price, or rating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Why' matters:** Understanding *why* we use `key=` (instead of writing a custom comparator) reflects the **Strategy Pattern** in software engineering — separating the *what to sort on* from the *how to sort*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Approach 1 — Using `sorted()` with `lambda` (Recommended / KTU Standard)

This is the **canonical KTU board-exam answer**. Each step is shown explicitly.

### Step 1: Define the input list of tuples

```python
students = [('Rahul', 85), ('Anjali', 92), ('Vikram', 78), ('Sneha', 88)]
```

Each tuple follows the schema `(name: str, marks: int)`.

### Step 2: Define the key extractor function

```python
get_second = lambda item: item[1]
```

This lambda, when called as `get_second(('Rahul', 85))`, returns `85` — the marks.

### Step 3: Apply the sort

```python
sorted_students = sorted(students, key=get_second)
```

Python internally:
- Calls `get_second` on each tuple: `[85, 92, 78, 88]`.
- Sorts these key values: `[78, 85, 88, 92]`.
- Maps sorted keys back to original tuples, preserving tuple identity.

### Step 4: Display the result

```python
print("Original:", students)
print("Sorted by marks:", sorted_students)
```

### Step 5: Expected output

```
Original: [('Rahul', 85), ('Anjali', 92), ('Vikram', 78), ('Sneha', 88)]
Sorted by marks: [('Vikram', 78), ('Rahul', 85), ('Sneha', 88), ('Anjali', 92)]
```

### ✅ Complete, Executable Program

```python
# Program: Sort a list of tuples by the second element

def main():
    # Step 1: Initialize the list of tuples
    students = [
        ('Rahul', 85),
        ('Anjali', 92),
        ('Vikram', 78),
        ('Sneha', 88),
        ('Karthik', 85)   # Duplicate mark to test stability
    ]
    
    print("Original list of tuples:")
    print(students)
    
    # Step 2: Use sorted() with a lambda key
    sorted_students = sorted(students, key=lambda x: x[1])
    
    print("\nSorted by second element (ascending):")
    print(sorted_students)
    
    # Step 3: Bonus — descending order
    sorted_desc = sorted(students, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    
    print("\nSorted by second element (descending):")
    print(sorted_desc)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### Step 6: Trace-through Verification

Let us manually trace the sort:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &= [(\text{'Rahul'}, 85), (\text{'Anjali'}, 92), (\text{'Vikram'}, 78), (\text{'Sneha'}, 88), (\text{'Karthik'}, 85)] \\
\text{Key extraction} &= [85, 92, 78, 88, 85] \\
\text{Ascending sort of keys} &= [78, 85, 85, 88, 92] \\
\text{Stable mapping to tuples} &= [(\text{'Vikram'}, 78), (\text{'Rahul'}, 85), (\text{'Karthik'}, 85), (\text{'Sneha'}, 88), (\text{'Anjali'}, 92)]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability proof:** Both `('Rahul', 85)` and `('Karthik', 85)` have the same key `85`. In the output, `('Rahul', 85)` appears **before** `('Karthik', 85)` — exactly the order they appeared in the input. This is the **stable sort guarantee**.

## 🧪 Approach 2 — Using `list.sort()` (In-Place Variant)

```python
data = [('apple', 5), ('banana', 2), ('cherry', 8)]
data.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])   # Modifies 'data' directly
print(data)                      # [('banana', 2), ('apple', 5), ('cherry', 8)]
```

| Aspect | `sorted()` | `list.sort()` |
|---|---|---|
| Returns new list | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (returns `None`) |
| Modifies original | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Memory | O(n) extra | O(1) auxiliary |
| Use case | When original must be preserved | When memory is critical |

## 🧪 Approach 3 — Using `operator.itemgetter` (Advanced / Bonus Marks)

```python
from operator import itemgetter

students = [('Rahul', 85), ('Anjali', 92), ('Vikram', 78)]
sorted_students = sorted(students, key=itemgetter(1))
```

`itemgetter(1)` is **marginally faster** than `lambda x: x[1]` because it's implemented in C. Worth mentioning in viva for **bonus marks**.

## 🧪 Approach 4 — Using a Named Function (For Better Readability)

```python
def get_marks(student_tuple):
    """Extracts the second element (marks) from a student tuple."""
    return student_tuple[1]

students = [('Rahul', 85), ('Anjali', 92), ('Vikram', 78)]
sorted_students = sorted(students, key=get_marks)
```

This is **equivalent** in behavior to the lambda version but is preferred in production code for documentation purposes.

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Approaches

| Approach | Code Length | Performance | Readability | KTU Board Preference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sorted()` + `lambda` | 1 line | O(n log n) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | **Highest** |
| `list.sort()` + `lambda` | 1 line | O(n log n) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | High |
| `sorted()` + `itemgetter` | 1 line | O(n log n), slightly faster | ⭐⭐⭐ | Bonus |
| `sorted()` + named function | 3 lines | O(n log n) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Viva discussion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the `key` parameter in Python's `sorted()` function? Explain with an example where it is used to sort a list of tuples by the second element.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `key` parameter accepts a **callable** (typically a `lambda` function or a built-in selector like `itemgetter`) that is invoked once on every element of the iterable. The **return value** of this callable becomes the basis for comparison during sorting; the original elements are reordered accordingly, but the elements themselves are never modified.

**Example:**

```python
data = [('apple', 5), ('banana', 2), ('cherry', 8)]
result = sorted(data, key=lambda x: x[1])
print(result)   # [('banana', 2), ('apple', 5), ('cherry', 8)]
```

Here, `lambda x: x[1]` extracts the second element (the integer) of each tuple, and `sorted()` arranges the tuples in ascending order of that extracted value. **The original tuples are not transformed — only reordered.** *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between `sorted()` and `list.sort()` in Python. Which one is suitable when the original list must be preserved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `sorted()` | `list.sort()` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Built-in function | List method |
| Returns | New sorted list | `None` |
| Original list | **Unchanged** | **Modified in-place** |
| Memory | O(n) extra | O(1) auxiliary |

**Suitable choice when original must be preserved:** `sorted()` — because it returns a new list and leaves the original iterable untouched. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in this experiment:
> 1. **Forgetting the lambda** — writing `sorted(data, x[1])` instead of `sorted(data, key=lambda x: x[1])`. This raises a `NameError` because `x` is undefined.
> 2. **Using `list.sort()` and then printing the wrong variable** — `.sort()` returns `None`, so `print(data.sort(...))` prints `None`.
> 3. **Off-by-one error** — using `x[0]` instead of `x[1]` (sorts by first element, not second). Always re-read the question.
> 4. **Not showing the original list** — KTU examiners award marks for demonstrating that the input is preserved (when using `sorted()`).
> 5. **Forgetting stability discussion** — when ties exist, the answer is incomplete without mentioning Timsort's stable-sort guarantee.
> 6. **No input/output documentation** — Always include a sample run / trace in the answer book.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Write a Python program to sort a list of tuples based on the **second element** in each tuple. Demonstrate with a list of `(name, marks)` tuples containing at least 5 entries, and display both the original and sorted lists. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Extend the program to sort the same list in **descending order** by marks, and also verify the **stability** of Python's sort by including two students with identical marks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

```python
# Program: Sort a list of tuples by the second element (ascending)

def sort_by_second_element():
    # Input list of (name, marks) tuples
    students = [
        ('Rahul', 85),
        ('Anjali', 92),
        ('Vikram', 78),
        ('Sneha', 88),
        ('Karthik', 95)
    ]
    
    # Display original list
    print("Original list of tuples:")
    print(students)
    
    # Sort using sorted() with lambda key
    sorted_students = sorted(students, key=lambda x: x[1])
    
    # Display sorted list
    print("\nSorted by marks (ascending):")
    print(sorted_students)

sort_by_second_element()
```

**Output:**
```
Original list of tuples:
[('Rahul', 85), ('Anjali', 92), ('Vikram', 78), ('Sneha', 88), ('Karthik', 95)]

Sorted by marks (ascending):
[('Vikram', 78), ('Rahul', 85), ('Sneha', 88), ('Anjali', 92), ('Karthik', 95)]
```

**Explanation (for marks):**
- We define a list of 5 tuples where each tuple contains a string (name) and an integer (marks). *(1 mark)*
- The `lambda x: x[1]` function takes a tuple `x` and returns its second element (index 1). *(2 marks)*
- `sorted()` creates a new list, leaving the original unchanged. *(1 mark)*
- The result is a list ordered by marks in ascending order. *(1 mark)*
- Both original and sorted lists are displayed to demonstrate the transformation. *(1 mark)*
- The code is well-commented and uses a function wrapper for modularity. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```python
# Program: Sort in descending order and verify stability

def sort_descending_and_test_stability():
    # List with two students having identical marks (85) to test stability
    students = [
        ('Rahul', 85),
        ('Anjali', 92),
        ('Vikram', 78),
        ('Sneha', 88),
        ('Karthik', 95),
        ('Meera', 85)     # Duplicate mark with Meera appearing AFTER Rahul
    ]
    
    # Descending sort
    sorted_desc = sorted(students, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    
    print("Sorted by marks (descending):")
    print(sorted_desc)
    
    # Stability verification
    print("\n--- Stability Test ---")
    print("Input order of marks=85 students: Rahul, then Meera")
    print("Output order of marks=85 students:")
    for name, marks in sorted_desc:
        if marks == 85:
            print(f"  {name} ({marks})")

sort_descending_and_test_stability()
```

**Output:**
```
Sorted by marks (descending):
[('Karthik', 95), ('Anjali', 92), ('Sneha', 88), ('Rahul', 85), ('Meera', 85), ('Vikram', 78)]

--- Stability Test ---
Input order of marks=85 students: Rahul, then Meera
Output order of marks=85 students:
  Rahul (85)
  Meera (85)
```

**Explanation (for marks):**
- The `reverse=True` parameter reverses the sort order to descending. *(1 mark)*
- Two students (`Rahul` and `Meera`) have identical marks of 85. *(1 mark)*
- In the input, `Rahul` appears before `Meera`. *(1 mark)*
- In the descending output, `Rahul` still appears before `Meera` — proving **stability**. *(2 marks)*
- Python uses **Timsort**, which is a stable sort algorithm, so the relative order of equal-key elements is preserved. *(1 mark)*
- The code clearly demonstrates and verifies the stability property with explicit print statements. *(1 mark)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Explain the concept of a **lambda function** in Python. Write a program that uses `sorted()` with a lambda function to sort a list of tuples `(product, price)` by price, and also display the **top 3 cheapest products**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare the use of `lambda x: x[1]` with `operator.itemgetter(1)` as key functions. Show the output of both approaches on the same dataset and discuss which is more **efficient** and why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

```python
# Program: Use lambda with sorted() and find top 3 cheapest products

def find_cheapest_products():
    # Lambda function definition (anonymous, single-expression)
    # A lambda is a small, unnamed function defined with: lambda args: expression
    
    products = [
        ('Laptop', 55000),
        ('Headphones', 1500),
        ('Mouse', 500),
        ('Keyboard', 1200),
        ('Monitor', 12000),
        ('Webcam', 2500)
    ]
    
    # Sort by price (second element) using lambda
    sorted_by_price = sorted(products, key=lambda x: x[1])
    
    print("All products sorted by price (ascending):")
    for product, price in sorted_by_price:
        print(f"  {product}: Rs. {price}")
    
    # Extract top 3 cheapest using list slicing [0:3]
    cheapest_three = sorted_by_price[:3]
    
    print("\nTop 3 cheapest products:")
    for product, price in cheapest_three:
        print(f"  {product}: Rs. {price}")

find_cheapest_products()
```

**Output:**
```
All products sorted by price (ascending):
  Mouse: Rs. 500
  Keyboard: Rs. 1200
  Headphones: Rs. 1500
  Webcam: Rs. 2500
  Monitor: Rs. 12000
  Laptop: Rs. 55000

Top 3 cheapest products:
  Mouse: Rs. 500
  Keyboard: Rs. 1200
  Headphones: Rs. 1500
```

**Explanation (for marks):**
- A **lambda function** is an anonymous, single-expression function with the syntax `lambda arguments: expression`. *(1 mark)*
- It can have any number of arguments but only **one expression**, which is implicitly returned. *(1 mark)*
- `lambda x: x[1]` extracts the price from each `(product, price)` tuple. *(2 marks)*
- `sorted_by_price[:3]` uses **list slicing** to get the top 3 cheapest items. *(2 marks)*
- The output is formatted with f-strings for readability. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```python
# Program: Compare lambda vs itemgetter for sorting tuples

from operator import itemgetter

def compare_approaches():
    products = [
        ('Laptop', 55000),
        ('Mouse', 500),
        ('Monitor', 12000)
    ]
    
    # Approach 1: lambda
    sorted_lambda = sorted(products, key=lambda x: x[1])
    
    # Approach 2: itemgetter
    sorted_itemgetter = sorted(products, key=itemgetter(1))
    
    print("Sorted with lambda:    ", sorted_lambda)
    print("Sorted with itemgetter:", sorted_itemgetter)
    
    # Performance comparison using timeit
    import timeit
    
    lambda_time = timeit.timeit(
        stmt="sorted(products, key=lambda x: x[1])",
        setup="products=[('Laptop',55000),('Mouse',500),('Monitor',12000)]",
        number=100000
    )
    
    ig_time = timeit.timeit(
        stmt="from operator import itemgetter; sorted(products, key=itemgetter(1))",
        setup="products=[('Laptop',55000),('Mouse',500),('Monitor',12000)]",
        number=100000
    )
    
    print(f"\nLambda time:   {lambda_time:.4f} seconds")
    print(f"itemgetter time: {ig_time:.4f} seconds")
    print(f"itemgetter is faster by ~{(lambda_time - ig_time)/lambda_time * 100:.2f}%")

compare_approaches()
```

**Comparison Table (for the answer book):**

| Aspect | `lambda x: x[1]` | `operator.itemgetter(1)` |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Pure Python | Implemented in **C** (faster) |
| Readability | Highly readable | Requires import, slightly cryptic |
| Flexibility | Can contain any expression | Only index/attribute access |
| Speed | Slower (function call overhead) | **Faster** (C-level dispatch) |
| KTU preference | **Yes** (taught in syllabus) | Bonus / advanced mention |

**Explanation (for marks):**
- `lambda x: x[1]` and `itemgetter(1)` produce **identical** sorting results. *(1 mark)*
- `itemgetter` is implemented in C and is therefore **faster** for large datasets. *(2 marks)*
- `lambda` is more **flexible** — it can encode complex expressions, not just indexing. *(1 mark)*
- For KTU board exams, the lambda approach is **preferred** because it directly reflects the syllabus. *(1 mark)*
- Both approaches preserve **stability**. *(1 mark)*
- The `timeit` module is used for empirical benchmarking. *(1 mark)*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sort List of Tuples by 2nd Element**

- ✅ The **canonical one-liner** is: `sorted(data, key=lambda x: x[1])` — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ `x[1]` refers to the **second element** (zero-indexed). `x[0]` is the first.
- ✅ `sorted()` returns a **new list**; `list.sort()` sorts **in-place** and returns `None`.
- ✅ Python's sort is **stable** — equal keys retain their input order (Timsort algorithm).
- ✅ Default sort order is **ascending**; use `reverse=True` for descending.
- ✅ The `key` parameter is called **once per element** — keys are cached, not recomputed during comparison.
- ✅ Time complexity is **O(n log n)**; space is **O(n)** for `sorted()`, **O(1)** auxiliary for `.sort()`.
- ✅ `operator.itemgetter(1)` is a faster alternative to `lambda x: x[1]` (C-implemented).
- ✅ Tuples are **immutable** — sorting creates a new ordered list; tuples themselves are never modified.
- ✅ For multi-level sort (e.g., sort by second, then by first for ties), use: `key=lambda x: (x[1], x[0])`.
- ✅ Always **display the original list** in the answer to prove the input was preserved (when using `sorted()`).
- ✅ For KTU lab records: include a **trace table**, **algorithm**, **program**, **output**, and **conclusion**.
- ✅ **Common viva question:** *"Why use `key=` instead of writing a custom comparator like in C++?"* — Answer: Python's `key` separates extraction from comparison, is more efficient (called once per element), and is more readable.
- ✅ **Bonus viva:** *"What happens if the second elements are of mixed types (e.g., int and str)?"* — Answer: Python raises a `TypeError` at comparison time, since `<` is not defined between `int` and `str`.
- ✅ **Advanced viva:** *"Can you sort by the second element of the second tuple in a nested list?"* — Answer: `key=lambda x: x[1][1]` — chained indexing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Python program to count the occurrences of each word in a given text and store the result in a dictionary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Python program to count the occurrences of each word in a given text and store the result in a dictionary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCCAL308 – Python Programming Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, a program that "counts the occurrences of each word in a given text and stores the result in a dictionary" is formally classified as a **Text Frequency Analysis** problem implemented using a **Hash Map** data structure. In Python, the built-in `dict` (dictionary) type is the canonical implementation of a hash map, providing average-case **O(1)** time complexity for insertion and lookup operations.

Formally, given an input string `T` containing `n` whitespace-separated tokens, the objective is to compute the function:

$$f: \text{Token} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$$

such that for every unique word `w` in `T`, the dictionary entry `result[w]` equals the total number of times `w` appears in `T`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO2 – K3 Level, Apply):**
> This experiment directly evaluates the Course Outcome *“Apply Python data structures (list, tuple, set, dictionary) and string manipulation techniques to solve real-world text processing problems.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Classroom Attendance Register

Imagine a school teacher who walks into a classroom and calls out the **name of every student** in sequence while noting down a tally mark next to each name in a register. By the end of the roll call:

- The **left column of the register** contains every *unique name* the teacher has heard (the **dictionary keys**).
- The **right column** contains the number of times each name was called (the **dictionary values**).
- The teacher does not pre-allocate space for every name in the alphabet — she **creates a new row the moment a new name is spoken** (this is *dynamic key insertion* in a hash map).

The Python `dict` works exactly the same way: every new word encountered becomes a *new key*, and the counter is *incremented* if the key already exists. The total **time complexity is O(n)** for `n` words, because each lookup and insertion is amortized **O(1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Lab Exam:**
> A dictionary in Python is an **unordered** (prior to 3.7) / **insertion-ordered** (from 3.7+) collection of **key–value pairs** enclosed in curly braces `{ }`, where keys must be **unique** and **immutable** (strings, numbers, tuples), while values can be of **any type**.

## 1.3 Python Primitives Required for This Experiment

| Primitive | Role in the Program | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `input()` | Accepts a text string from the user | `text = input("Enter text: ")` |
| `.split()` | Tokenizes the string into a list of words | `"I love Python".split()` → `['I', 'love', 'Python']` |
| `dict` | Stores word → count mapping | `{'the': 3, 'a': 2}` |
| `.lower()` | Normalizes words (case-insensitivity) | `"Hello".lower()` → `'hello'` |
| `str.strip(punct)` | Removes surrounding punctuation | `'"hello,"'.strip('",.')` → `'hello'` |
| `dict.get(key, default)` | Safe retrieval with a default value | `d.get('cat', 0) + 1` |
| `collections.Counter` | High-level specialized container (bonus) | `Counter(words)` |

## 1.4 Sample Input / Output Contract (KTU Lab Record Format)

```
Enter a line of text: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the
{'the': 3, 'quick': 1, 'brown': 1, 'fox': 1, 'jumps': 1, 'over': 1, 'lazy': 1, 'dog': 1}
```

This output format is the **exact deliverable** expected in your KTU lab record and viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Decomposition

The word-counting algorithm can be broken into **five crisp operational phases**. Every KTU examiner expects the student to articulate these phases in the viva.

### Phase 1 — Input Acquisition
The program reads a single line (or multi-line) text from standard input using `input()`. The string is captured in its raw form (with original casing and punctuation).

### Phase 2 — Normalization
Raw text contains noise: capital letters, trailing punctuation, special characters, and extra whitespace. The normalization phase performs three critical transformations:

1. **Case Folding:** Convert the entire string to lowercase via `.lower()` so that `"The"` and `"the"` are counted as the same word.
2. **Punctuation Stripping:** Iterate over each character, retain only alphabets/digits/spaces, OR use `str.translate()` with a translation table.
3. **Whitespace Trimming:** Apply `.strip()` to remove leading/trailing whitespace.

### Phase 3 — Tokenization
The normalized string is split into a list of substrings (tokens) on whitespace using `.split()`. By default, `.split()` already collapses runs of whitespace, so it elegantly handles tabs and multiple spaces.

### Phase 4 — Counting Loop
A `for` loop iterates over the token list. For each token `w`:
- If `w` is **not yet** in the dictionary, initialize `result[w] = 1`.
- If `w` **is already** present, increment: `result[w] += 1`.

A more **Pythonic, KTU-preferred** form is:
```python
result[w] = result.get(w, 0) + 1
```
which fuses the "check then insert/increment" into a single statement.

### Phase 5 — Display / Return
The final dictionary is printed using `print(result)` for the **default `repr` form**, or formatted with a loop for **human-readable column output** (preferred in KTU records).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **definitive reference** you should memorize for the KTU lab exam and viva.

| # | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | String Tokenization | `tokens = text.split()` | Splits on whitespace → list of words | $O(n)$ where $n = $ length of text |
| 2 | Case Normalization | `text.lower()` | Unifies casing | $O(n)$ |
| 3 | Punctuation Removal | `text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))` | Strips punctuation chars | $O(n)$ |
| 4 | Dictionary Initialization | `d = {}` | Empty hash map | $O(1)$ |
| 5 | Safe Get-and-Increment | `d[w] = d.get(w, 0) + 1` | Atomic update | $O(1)$ amortized |
| 6 | Manual Initialization | `if w in d: d[w]+=1 else: d[w]=1` | Two-branch update | $O(1)$ amortized |
| 7 | `Counter` (High-level) | `from collections import Counter; c = Counter(tokens)` | One-line solution | $O(n)$ |
| 8 | Display Dictionary | `for k, v in d.items(): print(k, ":", v)` | Pretty output | $O(k)$ where $k=$ unique words |
| 9 | Sort by Frequency | `sorted(d.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)` | Frequency-ranked list | $O(k \log k)$ |
| 10 | Total Time Complexity | $T(n) = n \cdot O(1) = O(n)$ | Whole-program runtime | $O(n)$ |
| 11 | Total Space Complexity | $S(n) = O(k)$ | where $k$ = number of unique words | $O(k)$ |

> **Note on Punctuation-Free Counting:** If you remove punctuation **before** tokenization, use `str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation)` with `str.translate()`. If you remove punctuation **after** tokenization, use `word.strip(string.punctuation)` inside the counting loop.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This experiment is the **fundamental building block** of:

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Building **bag-of-words** and **TF-IDF** vectors for spam filters, sentiment classifiers, and chatbots.
- **Search Engine Indexing:** Google-style **inverted indexes** that map every term to the documents containing it.
- **Plagiarism Detection:** Computing **word frequency vectors** to compute **cosine similarity** between two documents.
- **Compilers:** Building **symbol tables** that record how many times each identifier (variable, function name) is used.
- **Bioinformatics:** Counting **k-mer frequencies** in DNA sequences (where "words" are nucleotide substrings of length $k$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** If a KTU viva examiner asks *"Where is this used in industry?"*, the strongest single answer is: *“Inverted index construction for search engines like Elasticsearch, where every unique term maps to a postings list of document IDs and term frequencies.”*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Method 1 — The `if-else` Branching Approach (Foundational)

This is the **first method a KTU examiner expects** because it demonstrates raw understanding of dictionary state mutation.

```python
# Program 1: Word count using if-else branching
text = input("Enter a line of text: ")

# Normalize: lowercase and remove punctuation
import string
text = text.lower()
text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

# Tokenize
words = text.split()

# Counting
word_count = {}
for w in words:
    if w in word_count:
        word_count[w] += 1
    else:
        word_count[w] = 1

# Display
print("Word Occurrence Count:")
for word, count in word_count.items():
    print(f"{word} : {count}")
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Input:** `"The cat sat on the mat. The cat slept."`

**Step A — Lowercase:**
`"the cat sat on the mat. the cat slept."`

**Step B — Punctuation Removal** (the period after `mat.` is dropped):
`"the cat sat on the mat the cat slept"`

**Step C — Tokenize (`.split()`):**

$$
\text{words} = [\text{"the"}, \text{"cat"}, \text{"sat"}, \text{"on"}, \text{"the"}, \text{"mat"}, \text{"the"}, \text{"cat"}, \text{"slept"}]
$$

**Step D — Loop Iterations** (showing dictionary state after each step):

| Iteration | Word `w` | Check `w in word_count` | Action | Dictionary State After |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"the"` | False | `word_count["the"] = 1` | `{"the": 1}` |
| 2 | `"cat"` | False | `word_count["cat"] = 1` | `{"the": 1, "cat": 1}` |
| 3 | `"sat"` | False | `word_count["sat"] = 1` | `{"the": 1, "cat": 1, "sat": 1}` |
| 4 | `"on"`  | False | `word_count["on"]  = 1` | `{"the": 1, "cat": 1, "sat": 1, "on": 1}` |
| 5 | `"the"` | True  | `word_count["the"] = 2` | `{"the": 2, "cat": 1, "sat": 1, "on": 1}` |
| 6 | `"mat"` | False | `word_count["mat"] = 1` | `{"the": 2, "cat": 1, "sat": 1, "on": 1, "mat": 1}` |
| 7 | `"the"` | True  | `word_count["the"] = 3` | `{"the": 3, "cat": 1, "sat": 1, "on": 1, "mat": 1}` |
| 8 | `"cat"` | True  | `word_count["cat"] = 2` | `{"the": 3, "cat": 2, "sat": 1, "on": 1, "mat": 1}` |
| 9 | `"slept"` | False | `word_count["slept"] = 1` | `{"the": 3, "cat": 2, "sat": 1, "on": 1, "mat": 1, "slept": 1}` |

**Final Output:**
```
the : 3
cat : 2
sat : 1
on  : 1
mat : 1
slept : 1
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Method 2 — The `dict.get()` Pythonic Approach (Recommended for KTU)

This is the **preferred method** for full marks in KTU lab exams — it is **shorter, more efficient, and demonstrates Pythonic fluency**.

```python
# Program 2: Word count using dict.get()
text = input("Enter a line of text: ").lower()
import string
text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

word_count = {}
for w in text.split():
    word_count[w] = word_count.get(w, 0) + 1

print("Word Occurrence Count:", word_count)
```

### Derivation of the Single-Line Update Formula

We want a single expression that:
- Returns the current count of `w` if it exists,
- Returns 0 if it does not,
- Then adds 1 to the result and writes it back to `word_count[w]`.

Step 1 — Safe retrieval:
$$\text{current} = \text{word\_count.get}(w, 0)$$

Step 2 — Increment:
$$\text{new\_value} = \text{current} + 1$$

Step 3 — Atomic write-back:
$$\text{word\_count}[w] = \text{new\_value}$$

Fused into one line:
$$\text{word\_count}[w] = \text{word\_count.get}(w, 0) + 1$$

This avoids the `KeyError` that would occur on a naïve `word_count[w] + 1` for a first-seen word.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Method 3 — The `collections.Counter` High-Level Approach (Bonus, Viva Gold)

```python
# Program 3: Word count using collections.Counter
from collections import Counter
import string

text = input("Enter a line of text: ").lower()
text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

word_count = dict(Counter(text.split()))
print("Word Occurrence Count:", word_count)
```

`Counter` is a **subclass of `dict`** specifically optimized for counting hashable objects. It internally implements exactly the same `get(w, 0) + 1` logic but in C, making it ~3–5× faster on large corpora.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Three Methods

| Criterion | Method 1 (`if-else`) | Method 2 (`dict.get`) | Method 3 (`Counter`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lines of code (logic) | 4 | 1 | 1 |
| Beginner-friendliness | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |
| Pythonic idiomaticity | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Performance (small input) | Fast | Fast | Fastest |
| Performance (large input) | Slower | Fast | Fastest (C-implemented) |
| KTU Viva Impressiveness | Good | Excellent | Outstanding |
| Customization (e.g., sort by freq) | Manual | Manual | `.most_common(n)` built-in |
| KTU Recommended Marks | 9/10 | 10/10 | Bonus 1–2 marks |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Counting on a Multi-Sentence Paragraph

**Input paragraph:**
```
"Python is easy. Python is powerful. Python is popular."
```

**Step 1 — Lowercase:** `"python is easy. python is powerful. python is popular."`

**Step 2 — Remove punctuation:** `"python is easy python is powerful python is popular"`

**Step 3 — Tokenize:**
$$\text{words} = [\text{"python"}, \text{"is"}, \text{"easy"}, \text{"python"}, \text{"is"}, \text{"powerful"}, \text{"python"}, \text{"is"}, \text{"popular"}]$$

**Step 4 — Apply Method 2:**

| i | w | get(w, 0) | +1 | New dict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | python | 0 | 1 | {python:1} |
| 1 | is | 0 | 1 | {python:1, is:1} |
| 2 | easy | 0 | 1 | {python:1, is:1, easy:1} |
| 3 | python | 1 | 2 | {python:2, is:1, easy:1} |
| 4 | is | 1 | 2 | {python:2, is:2, easy:1} |
| 5 | powerful | 0 | 1 | {python:2, is:2, easy:1, powerful:1} |
| 6 | python | 2 | 3 | {python:3, is:2, easy:1, powerful:1} |
| 7 | is | 2 | 3 | {python:3, is:3, easy:1, powerful:1} |
| 8 | popular | 0 | 1 | {python:3, is:3, easy:1, powerful:1, popular:1} |

**Final dict:** `{'python': 3, 'is': 3, 'easy': 1, 'powerful': 1, 'popular': 1}`

## 3.6 Common Edge Cases (Must Test in Lab)

| Edge Case | Sample Input | Expected Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Empty string | `""` | Empty dict `{}` |
| Only whitespace | `"    "` | Empty dict `{}` |
| Mixed case | `"The THE tHe"` | All map to `the: 3` (after `.lower()`) |
| Trailing punctuation | `"hello, world!"` | Maps to `hello:1, world:1` |
| Numbers as words | `"I have 2 2 2 cats"` | `have:1, 2:3, cats:1` |
| Hyphenated words | `"well-known fact"` | After `translate` → `wellknown:1, fact:1` (hyphen stripped) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Diagram — The Word Counting Algorithm

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete control flow** of the algorithm, including normalization, tokenization, counting, and display. It is the kind of diagram a student should hand-draw in the lab record's "Algorithm" section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> input[/Read text from user/]
    input --> lower["text = text.lower()"]
    lower --> punct["Remove punctuation using translate"]
    punct --> split["words = text.split()"]
    split --> init["word_count = {}"]
    init --> loopCheck{words list empty?}
    loopCheck -- Yes --> display
    loopCheck -- No --> takeWord[/Take next word w/]
    takeWord --> getOp["current = word_count.get(w, 0)"]
    getOp --> update["word_count[w] = current + 1"]
    update --> loopCheck
    display[/Print word_count dict/] --> stop([Stop])

    classDef io fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef startend fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

    class input,display,takeWord io
    class lower,punct,split,init,getOp,update proc
    class loopCheck decision
    class start,stop startend
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Diamonds (orange) are decisions; rounded rectangles (blue) are I/O; rectangles (green) are processing steps; ovals (red) are start/stop.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Transformation Pipeline

The following block diagram shows how the raw text **transforms** through successive processing stages until the final frequency dictionary is produced. This is the recommended architecture diagram for the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A["Raw Text String"]
    end

    subgraph NORMALIZATION
        B["Lowercase Conversion"]
        C["Punctuation Removal"]
    end

    subgraph TOKENIZATION
        D["Whitespace Split"]
    end

    subgraph COUNTING
        E["Initialize Empty Dict"]
        F["For each token, get-or-zero + 1"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        G["Frequency Dictionary"]
        H["Pretty-Printed Report"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H

    classDef stage fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H stage
```

## 4.3 Memory Architecture — How the Dictionary Grows

The following diagram shows the **internal hash-map structure** of `word_count` after processing the paragraph *"Python is easy Python is powerful Python is popular"*. It illustrates how keys and values are stored.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HashMap["word_count Dictionary (Hash Map)"]
        K1["python"] --- V1["3"]
        K2["is"] --- V2["3"]
        K3["easy"] --- V3["1"]
        K4["powerful"] --- V4["1"]
        K5["popular"] --- V5["1"]
    end

    classDef key fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef val fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class K1,K2,K3,K4,K5 key
    class V1,V2,V3,V4,V5 val
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva Cue:** The hash function in CPython (the standard Python interpreter) computes a 64-bit hash of the key string, then masks the relevant bits to find the slot in the underlying hash table. This is why insertion and lookup are amortized $O(1)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Explain the role of the `dict.get(key, default)` method in a Python program that counts word occurrences. Why is it preferred over direct key access `dict[key]` for first-time insertions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `dict.get(key, default)` method retrieves the value associated with `key` in the dictionary, or returns `default` (here, `0`) if the key does not yet exist. It is preferred over `dict[key]` because direct key access raises a `KeyError` if the key is absent. In a word-counting program, when a new word is encountered for the first time, `word_count[w] + 1` would throw a `KeyError`. Using `word_count.get(w, 0) + 1` gracefully returns `0` for unseen words, allowing the increment to proceed without conditional checks. This makes the code **more concise, exception-free, and Pythonic** — qualities highly valued in KTU evaluations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**What is the time complexity of counting word occurrences using a Python dictionary? Justify your answer in terms of hash-map operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The total time complexity is **O(n)**, where `n` is the number of words (tokens) in the input text. Justification: For each of the `n` words, the algorithm performs exactly **one dictionary lookup or insertion**, which is an amortized **O(1)** operation due to the underlying hash-table structure. Therefore, the work done is `n × O(1) = O(n)`. The space complexity is **O(k)**, where `k` is the number of unique words, since only the dictionary needs to be stored in memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 3] (14 Marks)**

**Write a complete Python program to:**
**(a)** *(7 marks)* Accept a line of text from the user, convert it to lowercase, remove all punctuation, and split it into a list of words.
**(b)** *(7 marks)* Count the occurrences of each word using a dictionary and display the result in the format `word : count`, sorted alphabetically.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

```python
import string

# Step 1: Read input
text = input("Enter a line of text: ")

# Step 2: Convert to lowercase
text = text.lower()

# Step 3: Remove punctuation
text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

# Step 4: Split into words
words = text.split()

print("Tokenized words:", words)
```

**Explanation of steps (7 marks allocation):**
- (2 marks) Correct use of `input()` and `.lower()`.
- (2 marks) Use of `str.maketrans` and `str.translate` with `string.punctuation`.
- (2 marks) Correct application of `.split()` and storage into the `words` list.
- (1 mark) Proper indentation and `import string` declaration.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

```python
# Step 5: Count occurrences using dict.get()
word_count = {}
for w in words:
    word_count[w] = word_count.get(w, 0) + 1

# Step 6: Sort alphabetically and display
print("\nWord Occurrence Count (alphabetical):")
for word in sorted(word_count):
    print(f"{word} : {word_count[word]}")
```

**Explanation (7 marks allocation):**
- (3 marks) Correct counting loop with `dict.get(w, 0) + 1`.
- (2 marks) Use of `sorted(word_count)` to iterate in alphabetical order.
- (2 marks) Formatted output using f-string with `word` and `word_count[word]`.

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter a line of text: Hello, World! Hello Python. World is beautiful.

Word Occurrence Count (alphabetical):
beautiful : 1
hello : 2
is : 1
python : 1
world : 2
```

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 4] (14 Marks)**

**Write a complete Python program to:**
**(a)** *(7 marks)* Count the occurrences of each word in a given text using the `collections.Counter` class and store the result in a dictionary.
**(b)** *(7 marks)* Display the **top 3 most frequent words** along with their counts.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

```python
from collections import Counter
import string

# Step 1: Read and normalize input
text = input("Enter a line of text: ").lower()
text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))

# Step 2: Tokenize
words = text.split()

# Step 3: Use Counter
counter_obj = Counter(words)

# Step 4: Convert to plain dict
word_count = dict(counter_obj)

print("Word Count Dictionary:", word_count)
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) `from collections import Counter` and `import string`.
- (2 marks) Lowercase + punctuation removal pipeline.
- (2 marks) `Counter(words)` instantiation and conversion to `dict`.
- (1 mark) Proper output.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

```python
# Step 5: Top 3 most frequent words
print("\nTop 3 Most Frequent Words:")
for word, count in counter_obj.most_common(3):
    print(f"{word} : {count}")
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**
- (3 marks) Correct usage of `most_common(3)` method.
- (2 marks) Tuple unpacking `for word, count in ...`.
- (2 marks) Formatted output and handling of ties/edge cases.

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter a line of text: data science is fun data is powerful data drives decisions

Word Count Dictionary: {'data': 3, 'science': 1, 'is': 2, 'fun': 1, 'powerful': 1, 'drives': 1, 'decisions': 1}

Top 3 Most Frequent Words:
data : 3
is : 2
science : 1
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Examiner's Pitfall List:**

1. **Forgetting `.lower()` — Case Sensitivity Trap (–1 mark):** If the student does not lowercase, `"The"` and `"the"` are counted as different words. Examiners will test with mixed case and deduct a mark.
2. **Forgetting Punctuation Handling (–1 mark):** A naïve `text.split()` on `"hello, world!"` produces `['hello,', 'world!']` — the comma and exclamation are *part of the words*, inflating distinct counts. The student must remove punctuation *before* or *after* tokenization.
3. **KeyError on First Insertion (–1–2 marks):** Writing `word_count[w] += 1` inside the loop without first checking `if w in word_count` (or using `.get()`) raises a `KeyError` on the first occurrence of every word. This is the **#1 runtime error** in KTU lab submissions.
4. **Missing `import string` (–0.5 mark):** Using `string.punctuation` without importing the `string` module triggers a `NameError`.
5. **Improper Output Format (–1 mark):** Printing the dictionary as `print(word_count)` is acceptable but **printing only raw Python `repr`** without explanation may cost the "presentation" mark. Use a loop for column-style output.
6. **Not Testing Empty Input (–0.5 mark, viva):** If the viva examiner asks *"What if the user enters an empty string?"*, the correct answer is: *"`text.split()` returns an empty list, the for loop does not execute, and `word_count` remains `{}`."*
7. **Confusing `dict` with `list` (–2 marks):** Some students mistakenly use a list of `[word, count]` pairs and search linearly, which is O(n²). Always emphasize the **O(1) hash-map** advantage in the viva.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Entering the Lab!**

- ✅ The core problem is a **text frequency analyzer** built on a **Python dictionary (hash map)**.
- ✅ Standard pipeline: **input → normalize (lowercase + strip punctuation) → tokenize (split) → count loop → display**.
- ✅ Always use `word_count[w] = word_count.get(w, 0) + 1` to **avoid `KeyError`**.
- ✅ `string.punctuation` contains 32 characters: `!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~`.
- ✅ `str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation)` builds a translation table that maps every punctuation char to `None` (i.e., deletes it).
- ✅ `.split()` without arguments splits on **any whitespace** and discards empty tokens — perfect for tokenization.
- ✅ `dict` preserves **insertion order from Python 3.7+**, but for sorted output, use `sorted(dict)` or `sorted(dict.items())`.
- ✅ Time complexity of the entire program is **O(n)** for `n` words; space is **O(k)** for `k` unique words.
- ✅ `Counter` is a `dict` subclass optimized for counting — it provides `.most_common(n)` for free.
- ✅ Always **test with empty input, single word, repeated words, mixed case, and punctuation-laden text**.
- ✅ For KTU viva: be ready to explain **why** we use `.get()` over `[]`, and **why** dictionaries give O(1) lookup (hashing).
- ✅ Real-world use cases: **search engine indexing, NLP bag-of-words, plagiarism detection, compiler symbol tables**.
- ✅ Code must include a **docstring or comment header** with your name, roll number, experiment number, and date — KTU lab records require this metadata.

---

> **End of Notes — PCCAL308 / Module Experiment 2**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) syllabus. Aligns with CO2 (Apply data structures) and K3 (Apply) level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Python program to find all unique elements in a list
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Write a Python program to find all unique elements in a list # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCAL308 – Python Programming Lab**, the problem of finding *all unique elements in a list* is formally defined as a **set-theoretic deduplication operation** applied over a one-dimensional mutable sequence. Given an input list $L$ containing $n$ elements (which may include duplicates, mixed data types, or nested structures depending on problem constraints), the operation produces an output sequence $U$ such that: $$U = \{x \mid x \in L \text{ and } \text{count}(x \text{ in } L) \geq 1\}$$ where $U$ contains **each distinct element of $L$ exactly once**. The original order of first appearance is referred to as the *order-preserving uniqueness property* in KTU lab evaluation rubrics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** > - **CO1**: Develop Python programs using conditional statements, loops, and data structures. > - **Bloom's Level**: Apply (Level 3) / Analyse (Level 4) > - **Concept Cluster**: List operations, membership testing, set conversion, traversal algorithms. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Hostel Warden Analogy 🏨** > > Imagine a hostel warden calling a morning roll-call from a register that has names written in a messy, repetitive way: > - Register: `["Anu", "Ben", "Anu", "Ciya", "Ben", "Diya", "Anu"]` > - The warden wants to know: *"How many *different* students are on my list?"* > - The warden reads down the list and writes each name on a separate "unique" board **only the first time** the name is heard. If a name repeats, the warden simply ignores it. > - The final "unique board" reads: `["Anu", "Ben", "Ciya", "Diya"]` — each name appears **exactly once**, in the same order as it was first spoken. > > This is exactly what your Python program must do. The list is the messy register, and your program is the warden with a perfectly sharp memory. The **core computational metric** here is: - **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ (linear in the length of the list) - **Space Complexity:** $O(k)$ where $k$ is the number of unique elements ($k \leq n$) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the Operational Logic The problem of identifying unique elements in a Python list can be decomposed into **four distinct, sequential reasoning steps**. KTU examiners frequently award partial marks (typically 2 out of 7) for clearly articulating this logic before writing code. - **Step 1 — Input Acquisition & Validation:** Read the list from the user. Validate that the input is a non-null iterable. In KTU lab exams, you may receive input as a space-separated string (e.g., `"10 20 10 30"`) which must be converted via `input().split()` followed by type casting. - **Step 2 — Storage Initialization:** Create an empty container (`unique_list = []` or `seen = set()`) that will hold the elements we have encountered so far. - **Step 3 — Sequential Traversal & Membership Test:** Iterate over every element `x` in the input list. For each `x`, check whether it has *already been recorded* in the storage container. The membership operator `in` is the heart of this step and executes in **amortized $O(1)$** time when used with a `set`. - **Step 4 — Conditional Append / Collection:** - If `x` is **not** already in the storage, append it to the unique list (order-preserving method) **OR** add it to a set (unordered but faster method). - If `x` **is** already present, skip it (do nothing). ## 2.2 The Three Canonical KTU-Approved Approaches KTU 2024 Scheme lab rubrics recognize **three** valid implementation strategies, each carrying different marks for *efficiency analysis* and *code elegance*. ### 2.2.1 Approach 1: `set()` Conversion (Naïve / Idiomatic) This approach exploits Python's built-in `set` data type, which is mathematically defined to store only **distinct, hashable elements**. ```python my_list = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5] unique = list(set(my_list)) print(unique) # Order NOT guaranteed ``` ### 2.2.2 Approach 2: Order-Preserving Loop with List This is the **exam-preferred** approach in KTU 2024 lab evaluations because it demonstrates explicit understanding of *membership testing* and *traversal logic*. ```python my_list = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5] unique = [] for x in my_list: if x not in unique: unique.append(x) print(unique) # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] ``` ### 2.2.3 Approach 3: `dict.fromkeys()` Trick (Pythonic) A lesser-known but elegant method that leverages dictionary key uniqueness **and** preserves insertion order (a property guaranteed from Python 3.7+). ```python my_list = [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5] unique = list(dict.fromkeys(my_list)) print(unique) # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] ``` ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Method | Order Preserved? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | KTU 2024 Recommended? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | `list(set(L))` | ❌ No | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅ (for short scripts) | | `for` loop + `list` | ✅ Yes | $O(n^2)$ worst-case | $O(k)$ | ✅✅ (preferred for viva) | | `for` loop + `set` | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅✅✅ (most efficient) | | `dict.fromkeys(L)` | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅ (Pythonic alternative) | | `collections.OrderedDict` | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⚠️ (legacy, not preferred) | | `numpy.unique()` | ❌ No (ascending) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ❌ (external library) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > This exact operation is the backbone of **ETL pipelines** in data engineering, **deduplication stages** in distributed databases (Apache Spark's `distinct()` transformation is implemented on this principle), and **feature engineering** in machine learning where duplicate categorical labels must be collapsed before one-hot encoding. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Master Algorithm (Order-Preserving, Set-Aided) This is the **flagship implementation** that KTU 2024 scheme evaluators look for in the 14-mark lab question. We will derive every line of the program logically, with no skipped steps. ### 3.1.1 Mathematical Statement of the Problem Let the input list be: $$L = [l_1, l_2, l_3, \ldots, l_n]$$ The desired output $U$ is defined as: $$U = [l_i \mid i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \text{ and } l_i \notin \{l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_{i-1}\}]$$ In plain English: include element $l_i$ in $U$ **if and only if** it has not appeared in any earlier position. ### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Derivation of the Python Program **Line 1 — Program header & docstring** ```python # Program: Find all unique elements in a list # Course: PCCAL308 - Python Programming Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme) ``` **Line 2 — Input prompt** The user is asked to enter elements separated by spaces. We use `input()` which always returns a `str`, then `split()` (with no argument) which splits on **any whitespace**. ```python raw = input("Enter elements separated by space: ") ``` **Line 3 — Tokenization** The string `"10 20 10 30 20 40"` is split into the list `['10', '20', '10', '30', '20', '40']`. All tokens are still strings. ```python tokens = raw.split() ``` **Line 4 — Type casting to integers** We map each string token through `int()`. If a non-numeric token appears, Python raises `ValueError` — a point KTU examiners test in the viva. ```python numbers = [int(t) for t in tokens] ``` **Line 5 — Initialize the auxiliary set** We use a set named `seen` because set membership testing is **$O(1)$** (hash-table lookup), and a list `unique` to preserve order. ```python seen = set() unique = [] ``` **Lines 6–9 — The core traversal loop** ```python for x in numbers: if x not in seen: seen.add(x) unique.append(x) ``` The conditional logic is: $$ \text{action}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{add } x \text{ to } seen \text{ and append to } unique, & \text{if } x \notin seen \\ \text{skip } x, & \text{if } x \in seen \end{cases} $$ **Line 10 — Display the result** ```python print("Original list :", numbers) print("Unique list :", unique) ``` ### 3.1.3 Complete, Exam-Ready Program ```python # ============================================================ # Experiment 2: Find all unique elements in a list # KTU 2024 Scheme - PCCAL308 Python Programming Lab # ============================================================ # Step 1: Accept the list from the user raw = input("Enter integers separated by spaces: ").strip() # Step 2: Handle the edge case of empty input if not raw: print("No elements were entered. Exiting.") else: # Step 3: Tokenize and convert to integers numbers = [int(tok) for tok in raw.split()] # Step 4: Initialize the order-preserving uniqueness containers seen = set() # O(1) membership testing unique = [] # maintains first-seen order # Step 5: Traverse the list exactly once for x in numbers: if x not in seen: seen.add(x) # record that we have now seen x unique.append(x) # keep x in its original first-seen position # Step 6: Report the result print("Original list :", numbers) print("Unique list :", unique) print("Count :", len(unique), "unique element(s)") ``` ### 3.1.4 Worked Execution Trace (Dry Run) Let the user input be: `"5 3 5 7 3 9 1 7"`. The trace proceeds as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Iteration 1:} \quad & x = 5, \; 5 \notin seen \Rightarrow seen = \{5\}, \; unique = [5] \\ \textbf{Iteration 2:} \quad & x = 3, \; 3 \notin seen \Rightarrow seen = \{5, 3\}, \; unique = [5, 3] \\ \textbf{Iteration 3:} \quad & x = 5, \; 5 \in seen \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\ \textbf{Iteration 4:} \quad & x = 7, \; 7 \notin seen \Rightarrow seen = \{5, 3, 7\}, \; unique = [5, 3, 7] \\ \textbf{Iteration 5:} \quad & x = 3, \; 3 \in seen \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\ \textbf{Iteration 6:} \quad & x = 9, \; 9 \notin seen \Rightarrow seen = \{5, 3, 7, 9\}, \; unique = [5, 3, 7, 9] \\ \textbf{Iteration 7:} \quad & x = 1, \; 1 \notin seen \Rightarrow seen = \{5, 3, 7, 9, 1\}, \; unique = [5, 3, 7, 9, 1] \\ \textbf{Iteration 8:} \quad & x = 7, \; 7 \in seen \Rightarrow \text{skip} \end{aligned} $$ **Final output:** `Unique list : [5, 3, 7, 9, 1]` — the **5 unique elements** in their original first-appearance order. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Edge Case Behaviour > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 lab rubrics award **2 bonus marks** for handling these edge cases. The following table is exhaustive. | Input Scenario | Raw Input | Behaviour | Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | All unique | `"1 2 3 4"` | Every element is added | `[1, 2, 3, 4]` | | All duplicates | `"7 7 7 7"` | Only first `7` survives | `[7]` | | Single element | `"42"` | Loop runs once | `[42]` | | Empty input | `""` | Early exit branch | `No elements were entered.` | | Negative numbers | `"-3 5 -3 8"` | Handled by `int()` cast | `[-3, 5, 8]` | | Mixed case strings | `"apple Apple"` | Case-sensitive (different hashes) | `['apple', 'Apple']` | | Float elements | `"1.5 2.5 1.5"` | Cast to `float()` | `[1.5, 2.5]` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Control Flow of the Algorithm The following Mermaid diagram captures the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the unique-element extraction algorithm, from input acquisition to final output, including the decision diamond where the membership test is performed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[/Read raw input string/] B --> C{Is input empty?} C -- Yes --> D[/Display warning
Exit/] C -- No --> E[/Tokenize and
convert to int/] E --> F[/Initialize
seen = set
unique = list/] F --> G[/Iterate: for x in numbers/] G --> H{x not in seen?} H -- Yes --> I[/seen.add x
unique.append x/] H -- No --> J[/Skip x
continue loop/] I --> K{More elements?} J --> K K -- Yes --> G K -- No --> L[/Display unique list
and count/] L --> M([End]) D --> M ``` ## 4.2 Data State Evolution Diagram This diagram tracks how the `seen` set and `unique` list evolve **at every iteration** for the sample input `[5, 3, 5, 7, 3, 9, 1, 7]`. It is the most useful diagram for viva explanations. ```mermaid flowchart LR I1["Iter 1
x=5"] --> S1["seen={5}
unique=[5]"] I2["Iter 2
x=3"] --> S2["seen={5,3}
unique=[5,3]"] I3["Iter 3
x=5"] -. "skip (5 in seen)" .-> S2 I4["Iter 4
x=7"] --> S3["seen={5,3,7}
unique=[5,3,7]"] I5["Iter 5
x=3"] -. "skip (3 in seen)" .-> S3 I6["Iter 6
x=9"] --> S4["seen={5,3,7,9}
unique=[5,3,7,9]"] I7["Iter 7
x=1"] --> S5["seen={5,3,7,9,1}
unique=[5,3,7,9,1]"] I8["Iter 8
x=7"] -. "skip (7 in seen)" .-> S5 S5 --> FIN[/Final Output
[5, 3, 7, 9, 1]/] ``` ## 4.3 Method Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Find Unique Elements] --> M1[Method 1:
set conversion] ROOT --> M2[Method 2:
for + list] ROOT --> M3[Method 3:
for + set] ROOT --> M4[Method 4:
dict.fromkeys] M1 -.->|"Order lost
O(n)"| P1[Simple but
unreliable for viva] M2 -.->|"Order kept
O(n squared)"| P2[Clear logic
preferred for exams] M3 -.->|"Order kept
O(n)"| P3[Best balance
most efficient] M4 -.->|"Order kept
O(n)"| P4[Pythonic
less obvious] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Tip for Viva:** When asked *"Why a `set` for `seen` and a `list` for `unique`?"*, the correct answer is: *"A `set` provides $O(1)$ membership testing, while a `list` preserves insertion order — combining both gives us the best of speed and predictability."* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **Explain how Python's `set` data type can be used to extract unique elements from a list. Mention one limitation of this approach.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Python's `set` is an **unordered collection of unique, hashable elements**. When a list is passed to the `set()` constructor, all duplicate elements are automatically discarded because a set cannot store two identical items. **Limitation (1 mark):** The `set` data type does **not preserve the original order** of elements as they appeared in the source list. Therefore, converting `list(set(L))` is unsuitable when order matters. ```python # Demonstration L = [3, 1, 2, 1, 3] print(list(set(L))) # [1, 2, 3] - order may vary across runs ``` --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` **What is the time complexity of the membership test `x in my_list` when `my_list` is a Python list, and how does it change when `my_list` is a Python set?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - When `my_list` is a **list**, the expression `x in my_list` performs a **linear scan** from index 0 to the end, giving a time complexity of $\mathbf{O(n)}$ in the worst case. - When `my_list` is a **set**, Python computes a **hash** of `x` and looks it up in the hash table directly, giving an **amortized** time complexity of $\mathbf{O(1)}$. This is why experienced KTU candidates use a `set` as an auxiliary `seen` container while keeping the result in a `list` — combining the **speed of a set** with the **order-preserving property of a list**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Split:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You are expected to write the full algorithm, the Python code, a dry run, and a complexity analysis to score full marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU Model Question Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program that accepts a list of integers from the user, finds all **unique elements** while preserving their original order, and displays the count of duplicates removed. **(b) [7 Marks]** Modify the program to also display, for each duplicate element, the **number of times it appears** in the original list. #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks ```python # ============================================================ # Part (a): Unique elements with order preservation + count # ============================================================ raw = input("Enter integers separated by spaces: ").strip() if not raw: print("Empty input. Nothing to process.") else: original = [int(t) for t in raw.split()] seen = set() unique = [] for x in original: if x not in seen: seen.add(x) unique.append(x) duplicates_removed = len(original) - len(unique) print("Original list :", original) print("Unique list :", unique) print("Duplicates count:", duplicates_removed) ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter integers separated by spaces: 4 5 4 6 5 7 8 6 Original list : [4, 5, 4, 6, 5, 7, 8, 6] Unique list : [4, 5, 6, 7, 8] Duplicates count: 3 ``` **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** - Correct input parsing & type conversion → **2 marks** - Correct uniqueness algorithm → **3 marks** - Output formatting and edge case handling → **2 marks** #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks We extend the algorithm using `collections.Counter` for frequency analysis. ```python # ============================================================ # Part (b): Duplicate frequency analysis # ============================================================ from collections import Counter raw = input("Enter integers separated by spaces: ").strip() if not raw: print("Empty input.") else: original = [int(t) for t in raw.split()] freq = Counter(original) # O(n) frequency table seen = set() unique = [] for x in original: if x not in seen: seen.add(x) unique.append(x) print("Original list :", original) print("Unique list :", unique) print("\nElement-wise frequency:") print(f"{'Element':<10}{'Count':<10}{'Is duplicate?'}") print("-" * 32) for x in unique: is_dup = "Yes" if freq[x] > 1 else "No" print(f"{x:<10}{freq[x]:<10}{is_dup}") ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter integers separated by spaces: 4 5 4 6 5 7 8 6 Original list : [4, 5, 4, 6, 5, 7, 8, 6] Unique list : [4, 5, 6, 7, 8] Element-wise frequency: Element Count Is duplicate? -------------------------------- 4 2 Yes 5 2 Yes 6 2 Yes 7 1 No 8 1 No ``` **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** - Correct use of `Counter` → **2 marks** - Frequency table traversal & display formatting → **3 marks** - Logical integration with part (a) → **2 marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU Model Question Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program that reads a list of **strings** from the user and prints the unique strings in the order of their **last occurrence** in the list. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare, with example, the three approaches — `list(set(L))`, order-preserving loop with list, and `dict.fromkeys(L)` — in terms of order preservation and time complexity. #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks The trick here is to **reverse the list**, perform standard order-preserving deduplication, and then **reverse the result**. ```python # ============================================================ # Part (a): Unique strings by last-occurrence order # ============================================================ raw = input("Enter words separated by spaces: ").strip() if not raw: print("No input received.") else: words = raw.split() # keep as strings, no casting seen = set() unique_by_last = [] # Traverse in reverse to capture last-occurrence order for w in reversed(words): if w not in seen: seen.add(w) unique_by_last.append(w) unique_by_last.reverse() # restore left-to-right reading order print("Original list :", words) print("Unique (by last) :", unique_by_last) ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter words separated by spaces: apple banana apple cherry banana date Original list : ['apple', 'banana', 'apple', 'cherry', 'banana', 'date'] Unique (by last) : ['apple', 'cherry', 'banana', 'date'] ``` **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** - Correct reversed traversal logic → **3 marks** - Use of `set` for `seen` → **2 marks** - Final reversal and output → **2 marks** #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table with Example Input:** `L = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3, 5]` | Approach | Code Snippet | Output | Order Preserved? | Time Complexity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | | `list(set(L))` | `list(set(L))` | `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]` | ❌ No | $O(n)$ | | Order-preserving loop | `for x in L: if x not in U: U.append(x)` | `[3, 1, 4, 5, 9, 2, 6]` | ✅ Yes | $O(n^2)$ | | `dict.fromkeys(L)` | `list(dict.fromkeys(L))` | `[3, 1, 4, 5, 9, 2, 6]` | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ | **Verdict (2 marks):** For KTU 2024 lab submissions, the **order-preserving loop using a `set` as auxiliary storage** is the recommended approach — it is intuitive, demonstrably correct, and runs in optimal $O(n)$ time. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in PCCAL308** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert input strings to integers** — `input().split()` returns a list of *strings*, so `10 == 10` fails for `"10" == "10"` comparisons across types. Always cast explicitly. > 2. **Not handling empty input** — If the user simply presses Enter, `raw.split()` returns `[]` and your loop will produce empty output. KTU 2024 rubrics dock **1 mark** for missing this edge case. > 3. **Using `set` for the final output without a comment** — Examiners interpret this as *not understanding order preservation*. Always either use a list or add a comment justifying the unordered output. > 4. **Hardcoding the input list** — `my_list = [1, 2, 3]` inside the program is an instant **−2 mark** penalty. The list must come from `input()`. > 5. **Missing the dry-run / trace table** — For 14-mark questions, KTU 2024 requires a handwritten or printed trace table showing the state of `seen` and `unique` after each iteration. Skipping it costs **2 marks** even if the code is perfect. > 6. **Confusing `append` vs `extend` vs `add`** — `list.append(x)` adds one element; `set.add(x)` adds one element. Using `append` on a set raises `AttributeError`. Examiners *do* test this in viva. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for PCCAL308 Module Experiment 2** > > - ✅ The **canonical algorithm** is: *traverse the list once, use a `set` for fast membership testing, and a `list` to preserve first-seen order.* > - ✅ **Three valid methods** in KTU 2024: `set()` conversion, order-preserving `for` loop, and `dict.fromkeys()`. Know all three for viva. > - ✅ **Time complexity of the optimal approach** is $O(n)$ — be ready to derive this in the viva. > - ✅ The membership operator `in` is $O(1)$ on `set` and $O(n)$ on `list`. Mixing them is the key insight. > - ✅ **Always validate input** — handle the empty string and non-numeric cases for full marks. > - ✅ **Always provide a dry-run trace** for any 14-mark question; it is the single biggest differentiator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer. > - ✅ The output `list(set(L))` is **faster to type but loses order** — never use it in viva unless explicitly told order does not matter. > - ✅ The `collections.Counter` class is the cleanest way to compute duplicate frequencies for follow-up questions. > - ✅ **Pythonic one-liner:** `unique = list(dict.fromkeys(L))` — memorise this; it impresses examiners. > - ✅ Real-world impact: this operation is the foundation of **data deduplication, ETL pipelines, and database `DISTINCT` queries**.

topics.title: Write a Python program to create a set of squares of all even numbers between m and n using set comprehension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Python program to create a set of squares of all even numbers between m and n using set comprehension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Set Comprehension** is a concise, elegant, and mathematically inspired syntactic construct in Python used to construct a new `set` object by applying an expression to each element of one or more iterable sequences, optionally filtered by a predicate condition. It is structurally analogous to **Set Builder Notation** used in discrete mathematics.

The general canonical form of a set comprehension is:

$$S = \{\ \text{expression}(x)\ \text{for}\ x \in \text{iterable}\ \text{if}\ \text{condition}(x)\ \}$$

In the context of this experiment, the formal problem statement is:

> Given two integer boundaries $m$ and $n$ (where $m < n$), construct a Python set containing the squares ($x^2$) of every **even** integer $x$ lying strictly within the open interval $(m, n)$.

Mathematically, the target set is:

$$S = \{\ x^2 \ :\ x \in \mathbb{Z},\ m < x < n,\ x \bmod 2 \equiv 0\ \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCAL308 - Module 2):** Set comprehension is explicitly listed as a core learning outcome under *Python Data Structures - Sets*. The university expects students to demonstrate proficiency in writing one-line comprehensions, predicting output, and modifying expressions to filter or transform iterable data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **quality inspector in a factory** standing at the entrance of a conveyor belt:

1. Items (raw materials) arrive one by one on the belt — these are your **iterables** (the numbers from $m$ to $n$).
2. You have a **magnet that only attracts iron** — this is your **filter condition** (e.g., "only even numbers").
3. After picking up the iron pieces, you place each one inside a **box that doubles the size of whatever you put in** — this is your **transformation** (squaring the value).
4. Finally, you pour all the resulting objects into a **magic bag that automatically removes duplicates** — this is the **set** data structure.

A set comprehension does **all four steps in a single, compact line of code**, much like a fully automated assembly line replacing a manual four-step process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Every Comprehension:**
> 1. **Transformation Expression** — what to compute for each element.
> 2. **Iteration Clause** — the source iterable to traverse.
> 3. **Filter Predicate** *(optional)* — the condition an element must satisfy to be included.

</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Syntax

Python's set comprehension is governed by a strict micro-grammar. Each token plays a deterministic role in the iteration pipeline.

| Token Component | Mandatory? | Role in Execution Pipeline |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| `{` and `}` | **Yes** | Demarcates the comprehension as a **set** (curly braces are mandatory; not a generator, not a dict). |
| `expression` | **Yes** | The value inserted into the set for every qualifying element. |
| `for x in iterable` | **Yes** | The iteration clause that binds the loop variable. |
| `if condition` | No | A guard clause; elements failing the test are silently dropped. |
| `for x in ... for y in ...` | No | Nested iteration; produces the Cartesian product. |

The general KTU-tested form for this experiment is:

```python
my_set = {x**2 for x in range(m, n) if x % 2 == 0}
```

## 2.2 Why Squaring? Why Even Filtering? — The "How" & "Why"

* **Why the `range(m, n)` boundary?** Python's `range()` is **half-open**, meaning it yields integers from $m$ up to, but **not including**, $n$. This perfectly matches the strict inequality $m \le x < n$ required by the problem. KTU examiners frequently test whether students mistakenly use `range(m, n+1)` — that is a classic mark-deduction trap.
* **Why `x % 2 == 0`?** The modulus operator returns the remainder. An even number is, by definition, an integer $x$ such that $x = 2k$ for some $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. Therefore, $x \bmod 2 \equiv 0$. This is the canonical, Pythonic, and most readable evenness test.
