- **Pythonic**: leverages Python's file iterator protocol.
- **Handles large files** (e.g., 10 GB log files) without RAM exhaustion.

---

## 3.3 Method 3 — Using `readlines()` and `writelines()`

```python
# Method 3: Using readlines() and writelines()
# Suitable for small-to-medium files

with open("source.txt", "r") as src:
    lines = src.readlines()           # Returns list of lines

with open("destination.txt", "w") as dst:
    dst.writelines(lines)             # Writes each line from the list

print("File copied using readlines/writelines!")
```

### Trace Matrix

| Step | Memory Footprint | Disk Reads | Disk Writes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `readlines()` | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — all lines in RAM | 1 | 0 |
| `writelines()` | Same list reused | 0 | $n$ writes |

> [!NOTE]
> **`writelines()` does NOT add newline characters automatically.** The source file's existing `\n` characters are preserved within each string of the list, which is why this method "just works" for line-based copying.

---

## 3.4 Method 4 — Using the `shutil` Module (High-Level)

```python
# Method 4: Using shutil module (the most Pythonic way)
# Requires: import shutil

import shutil

# shutil.copyfile() copies contents only
shutil.copyfile("source.txt", "destination.txt")

# Or shutil.copy() copies contents + permissions
shutil.copy("source.txt", "destination_copy.txt")

print("File copied using shutil module!")
```

### Comparison: `shutil` Functions

| Function | Copies Content | Copies Permissions | Copies Metadata | Destination Must Be |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `shutil.copyfile()` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Filename only |
| `shutil.copy()` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Directory or filename |
| `shutil.copy2()` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Directory or filename |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Write a program to copy a file using the `shutil` module."* Method 4 is the **shortest, most elegant** answer — perfect for scoring the full 14 marks in ESE.

---

## 3.5 Method 5 — Using Command-Line via `os.popen()`

```python
# Method 5: Using OS shell command
# Demonstrates: cross-platform awareness

import os

# Unix/Linux/Mac version
os.popen("cp source.txt destination.txt")

# Windows version
# os.popen("copy source.txt destination.txt")

print("File copied using OS command!")
```

> [!WARNING]
> This method is **OS-dependent** and **not recommended** for production. It is included only for academic completeness — KTU has occasionally asked cross-platform questions.

---

## 3.6 Final Consolidated Reference Program (Best for KTU Exam)

```python
"""
Experiment 2 (Module 2): Copy contents of one text file to another
Course : PCCAL308 - Python Programming Lab
Scheme : KTU 2024
"""
import os
import shutil

SOURCE = "input.txt"
DEST   = "output.txt"

# ---------- Validate source exists ----------
if not os.path.exists(SOURCE):
    print(f"Error: '{SOURCE}' not found.")
    exit(1)

# ---------- Method A: read()/write() ----------
with open(SOURCE, "r") as src, open(DEST, "w") as dst:
    dst.write(src.read())

# ---------- Method B: line-by-line (commented for exam) ----------
# with open(SOURCE, "r") as src, open(DEST, "w") as dst:
#     for line in src:
#         dst.write(line)

# ---------- Method C: shutil (commented for exam) ----------
# shutil.copyfile(SOURCE, DEST)

print(f"'{SOURCE}' successfully copied to '{DEST}'.")

# ---------- Verification ----------
with open(DEST, "r") as f:
    print("--- Destination File Contents ---")
    print(f.read())
```

### Output Trace (Sample)

```
'input.txt' successfully copied to 'output.txt'.
--- Destination File Contents ---
Hello, KTU 2024!
Python Programming Lab - Module 2
File Copying Experiment
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — File Copy Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([User Runs Program]) --> B{Source File Exists?}
    B -- No --> Z([Display Error and Exit])
    B -- Yes --> C[Open Source in Read Mode]
    C --> D[Read Content - Whole or Line-by-Line]
    D --> E[Close Source File]
    E --> F[Open Destination in Write Mode]
    F --> G[Write Content to Destination]
    G --> H[Close Destination File]
    H --> I{Verify with Re-read?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Re-open Destination and Print]
    I -- No --> K([End of Program])
    J --> K
    Z --> K
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Interaction Between Python and OS

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Program
    participant P as Python open()
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant D as Disk Storage

    U->>P: open("source.txt", "r")
    P->>OS: sys call: open() - read
    OS->>D: locate file inode
    D-->>OS: file metadata + FD
    OS-->>P: file descriptor 3
    P-->>U: TextIOWrapper object

    U->>P: file.read()
    P->>OS: sys call: read()
    OS->>D: fetch bytes
    D-->>OS: raw bytes
    OS-->>P: byte stream
    P-->>U: decoded string

    U->>P: open("dest.txt", "w")
    P->>OS: sys call: open() - write
    OS->>D: create / truncate
    OS-->>P: file descriptor 4
    P-->>U: TextIOWrapper object

    U->>P: file.write(data)
    P->>OS: sys call: write()
    OS->>D: persist bytes
    OS-->>P: bytes written

    U->>P: file.close()
    P->>OS: sys call: close()
    OS->>D: flush buffers
    OS-->>P: FD released
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to Copy a Text File]) --> Q1{File Size?}
    Q1 -- Small under 1 MB --> Q2{Need Metadata?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M1[Use shutil.copy2]
    Q2 -- No --> M3[Use shutil.copyfile]
    Q1 -- Large over 100 MB --> M2[Use Line-by-Line Loop]
    Q1 -- Huge Gigabytes --> M3b[Use shutil.copyfileobj with Chunks]
    Q1 -- Cross-Platform Shell --> M4[Use os.popen or subprocess]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** When asked *"Which method would you use for a 50 GB log file?"*, answer: **Method 2 (line-by-line streaming)** or **`shutil.copyfileobj()` with a fixed chunk size of 8192 bytes**, because loading 50 GB into RAM will crash the program.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*What is the difference between `read()`, `readline()`, and `readlines()` methods in Python file handling? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Method | Syntax | Returns | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read()` | `f.read(n)` | String of `n` chars (or entire file) | High |
| `readline()` | `f.readline()` | Single line as string (with `\n`) | Low |
| `readlines()` | `f.readlines()` | List of all lines | High |

```python
f = open("data.txt", "r")
print(f.read(5))       # First 5 characters
print(f.readline())    # First line
print(f.readlines())   # List of all remaining lines
```

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 m) + Example (1 m) + Use-case comparison (1 m).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Why is it recommended to use the `with` statement when working with files in Python? What happens if you forget to close a file?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `with` statement is a **context manager** that **automatically closes the file** once the block exits, even if an exception is raised inside the block.

**Syntax:**
```python
with open("test.txt", "r") as f:
    data = f.read()
# File f is automatically closed here
```

**Consequences of NOT closing a file:**
1. **Resource leak** — File descriptor remains allocated.
2. **Data loss** — Data in the write buffer is not flushed to disk.
3. **File lock** — On Windows, the file remains locked, preventing other programs from accessing it.
4. **System slowdown** — Too many open FDs can exhaust the OS limit (typically 1024 on Linux).

**Mark Distribution:** `with` explanation (1 m) + Syntax (1 m) + Consequences (1 m).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Write a Python program to copy the contents of one text file to another. Use the `with` statement and handle the case where the source file does not exist using exception handling.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Full Program with Exception Handling**

**Model Solution:**

```python
# PCCAL308 - Experiment 2: File Copy with Exception Handling
# Question A - Part (a)

import os

source = "source.txt"
destination = "destination.txt"

try:
    # Check source existence
    if not os.path.isfile(source):
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Source file '{source}' not found.")

    # Open both files using 'with' context manager
    with open(source, "r") as src, open(destination, "w") as dst:
        # Read entire content
        content = src.read()
        # Write to destination
        dst.write(content)

    print(f"SUCCESS: '{source}' copied to '{destination}'.")

except FileNotFoundError as e:
    print(f"ERROR: {e}")
except IOError as e:
    print(f"I/O ERROR: {e}")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"UNEXPECTED: {e}")
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (a):**
- Correct use of `with` (2 m)
- `try-except` blocks (2 m)
- File mode selection (1 m)
- Output statement (1 m)
- Indentation & syntax (1 m)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Verification + Output**

**Model Solution:**

```python
# Part (b): Verification step
import os

# Display file sizes
src_size  = os.path.getsize(source)
dest_size = os.path.getsize(destination)

print(f"Source size      : {src_size} bytes")
print(f"Destination size : {dest_size} bytes")

if src_size == dest_size:
    print("VERIFIED: Files are identical in size.")
else:
    print("MISMATCH: Sizes differ!")

# Display content of destination
with open(destination, "r") as f:
    print("\n--- Contents of destination.txt ---")
    print(f.read())
```

**Sample Output:**

```
SUCCESS: 'source.txt' copied to 'destination.txt'.
Source size      : 156 bytes
Destination size : 156 bytes
VERIFIED: Files are identical in size.

--- Contents of destination.txt ---
Hello, KTU 2024!
Python Programming Lab - Module 2
File Copying Experiment
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**
- `os.path.getsize()` usage (2 m)
- Size comparison logic (2 m)
- Re-reading and displaying (2 m)
- Output formatting (1 m)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Explain five different methods to copy a text file to another file in Python. Compare their memory efficiency and use cases.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Five Methods with Code**

**Model Solution:**

**Method 1 — `read()` + `write()`**
```python
with open("a.txt", "r") as s, open("b.txt", "w") as d:
    d.write(s.read())
```
*Memory:* $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — entire file in RAM.

**Method 2 — `readlines()` + `writelines()`**
```python
with open("a.txt", "r") as s:
    lines = s.readlines()
with open("b.txt", "w") as d:
    d.writelines(lines)
```
*Memory:* $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — list of all lines.

**Method 3 — Line-by-line iteration**
```python
with open("a.txt", "r") as s, open("b.txt", "w") as d:
    for line in s:
        d.write(line)
```
*Memory:* $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only one line at a time.

**Method 4 — `shutil.copyfile()`**
```python
import shutil
shutil.copyfile("a.txt", "b.txt")
```
*Memory:* Optimized at C level — fastest.

**Method 5 — `shutil.copyfileobj()` with chunks**
```python
import shutil
with open("a.txt", "r") as s, open("b.txt", "w") as d:
    shutil.copyfileobj(s, d, length=8192)
```
*Memory:* $\mathcal{O}(8192)$ — fixed buffer.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis Table**

| Method | Memory | Speed | Best For | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `read()+write()` | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Fast | Small files | Most common in exams |
| `readlines()` | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Fast | Config files | Common |
| Line iteration | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Slower (many syscalls) | Huge files | Best practice |
| `shutil.copyfile()` | C-optimized | Fastest | Production code | Marks awarded |
| `shutil.copyfileobj()` | Fixed buffer | Fastest + scalable | Any size | Bonus marks |

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**
- Comparative table (3 m)
- Memory analysis (2 m)
- Use-case mapping (2 m)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to close files** — When using `open()` without `with`, if you skip `close()`, the write buffer is **not flushed**, and the destination file remains **empty**. This is the **#1 reason for zero output** in lab exams.
> 2. **Wrong mode** — Using `"r"` for the destination file raises `UnsupportedOperation: not writable`. Always use `"w"` or `"a"`.
> 3. **Adding extra newline** — Using `dst.write(line + "\n")` when iterating causes **double newlines** because `line` already contains `\n`.
> 4. **Path errors** — Hardcoded paths like `"C:\Users\test.txt"` fail because `\t` and `\U` are interpreted as escape sequences. Use **raw strings** `r"C:\Users\test.txt"` or forward slashes `"C:/Users/test.txt"`.
> 5. **Encoding errors** — Reading a UTF-8 file with default encoding on Windows (cp1252) can crash. Always specify `encoding="utf-8"`.
> 6. **Confusing `shutil.copy()` with `shutil.copyfile()`** — Examiners award full marks only for the **correct** function used in context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four-beat rhythm:** *Open → Read → Write → Close.*
- **Best practice:** Always use `with open(...) as f:` — automatic resource management.
- **Small files:** `f.read()` + `f.write()` is sufficient and most readable.
- **Large files:** Iterate `for line in src: dst.write(line)` to avoid memory overflow.
- **Production code:** Use `shutil.copyfile()` for one-liner elegance or `shutil.copyfileobj()` for chunked streaming.
- **Modes to remember:** `'r'` (read), `'w'` (write-truncate), `'a'` (append), `'b'` (binary), `'+'` (update).
- **Encoding:** Always specify `encoding="utf-8"` to avoid cross-platform issues.
- **Error handling:** Wrap file operations in `try-except FileNotFoundError, IOError`.
- **Verification:** Use `os.path.getsize()` or a re-read loop to confirm identical contents.
- **Viva favorites:** Difference between `read`/`readline`/`readlines`; why `with` is preferred; `shutil` vs `os`; handling binary vs text files.
- **Mark-winning tip:** Show **at least 2 methods** in your answer script and a **comparison table** — examiners reward breadth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Python program to merge the contents of two text files and write the result into a third file
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Python program to merge the contents of two text files and write the result into a third file

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Python Programming Lab (PCCAL308)**, a file merging operation is defined as a **sequential file I/O (Input/Output) procedure** in which the byte-stream contents of two or more source text files (`source_1.txt`, `source_2.txt`) are concatenated in a defined order and persisted as a single, unified content body within a destination text file (`merged.txt`). This operation is executed using Python's built-in `open()` built-in function, file mode specifiers, and the `read()` / `write()` file methods encapsulated within a `with` context manager to guarantee deterministic resource deallocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCAL308 - Module 2):** File handling in Python — Opening and closing files, reading/writing modes, `with` statement, file pointers, and `os` module utilities. Merging two files is a classic *application-level* lab problem that tests the learner's ability to combine **read mode ('r')** and **write mode ('w')** in a single coherent program.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Two Notebooks Into One" Metaphor**
> Imagine you have two physical notebooks:
> - **Notebook A** contains notes from Monday's class.
> - **Notebook B** contains notes from Tuesday's class.
>
> You are asked by your professor to **create a third notebook** (`merged.txt`) that contains *everything* from Notebook A **followed by** everything from Notebook B.
>
> What do you do?
> 1. You **open** Notebook A and read every page.
> 2. You **open** the new Notebook (the third one) and write down what you read from A.
> 3. You **close** Notebook A.
> 4. You **open** Notebook B and read every page.
> 5. You **open** the third notebook again (or keep it open) and write what you read from B.
> 6. You **close** both notebooks.
>
> That is *exactly* what the Python program does — except Python uses file objects, file pointers, and I/O buffers instead of hands and notebooks.

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

This experiment is a **high-frequency lab question** in the KTU continuous evaluation record and the university practical exam. It tests the following **Course Outcomes (COs)** as per the 2024 scheme:

| CO Code | Course Outcome Description | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Develop Python programs using file handling operations and string manipulation. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO3** | Implement modular solutions using functions, exception handling, and I/O streams. | Implement (L3) |

## 1.4 Key Entities in This Program

| Entity | Role in Program | KTU Naming Convention |
|---|---|---|
| `file1` | First source text file (input) | `file1.txt` |
| `file2` | Second source text file (input) | `file2.txt` |
| `file3` | Destination/merged file (output) | `merged.txt` |
| `mode 'r'` | Read mode (default; opens file for reading) | Source mode |
| `mode 'w'` | Write mode (creates/truncates file) | Destination mode |
| `with` | Context manager (auto-closes file) | Best practice |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

File merging in Python is a **three-stage sequential pipeline**: **Open → Read → Write**. Each stage has a specific method and mode. Below is the granular breakdown:

### Stage 1: **File Acquisition (Opening)**
- Python uses the built-in function `open(filepath, mode)` to acquire an OS-level file handle.
- The `mode` parameter is a **string literal** that dictates the permission and the file pointer's initial position.
- For *reading* source files, mode is `'r'` (text mode by default).
- For *writing* the merged file, mode is `'w'` (creates a new file or truncates an existing one).

### Stage 2: **Data Extraction (Reading)**
- `file_object.read()` returns the **entire content** of the file as a single `str` object.
- `file_object.readline()` returns **one line at a time** (useful for very large files).
- `file_object.readlines()` returns a **list of strings**, each representing a line (preserving `\n` delimiters).

### Stage 3: **Data Persistence (Writing)**
- `file_object.write(string)` writes the given string to the file. It does **not** append a newline automatically.
- `file_object.writelines(list_of_strings)` writes multiple strings from an iterable.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Required | How It Is Implemented in Python |
|---|---|---|
| Open file | OS needs a handle to access disk data | `f = open("file1.txt", "r")` |
| Read file | Load content from disk into RAM | `data = f.read()` |
| Write file | Persist combined content to a new file | `out.write(data1 + data2)` |
| Close file | Release OS resources and flush buffers | `f.close()` (or auto via `with`) |
| Use `with` | Guarantee closure even on exception | `with open(...) as f:` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table — File Modes in Python (KTU High-Yield)**

| Mode Symbol | Read | Write | Create New | Truncate | Pointer Position | Use Case |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| `'r'` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning | Default read mode |
| `'w'` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | Overwrite/merge destination |
| `'a'` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | End | Append mode |
| `'r+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning | Read + write (no truncate) |
| `'w+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | Read + write (truncate) |
| `'a+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | End | Read + append |
| `'rb'` | ✅ (bytes) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning | Binary read |
| `'wb'` | ❌ | ✅ (bytes) | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | Binary write |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Python Built-ins for File Merging**

| Function / Method | Syntax | Returns | Side Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `open()` | `open(file, mode)` | file object | Allocates OS handle |
| `f.read()` | `file.read(size=-1)` | `str` | Moves pointer to EOF |
| `f.readline()` | `file.readline(size=-1)` | `str` (one line) | Moves pointer to next `\n` |
| `f.readlines()` | `file.readlines()` | `list[str]` | Moves pointer to EOF |
| `f.write(s)` | `file.write(str)` | `int` (chars written) | Inserts at pointer |
| `f.writelines(lst)` | `file.writelines(iterable)` | `None` | Bulk write |
| `f.close()` | `file.close()` | `None` | Flushes & releases handle |
| `with ... as` | `with open(p,m) as f:` | context manager | Auto-close on exit |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the software industry, the *"merge two files"* pattern is foundational to:

1. **Log Aggregation Systems** — Merging log streams from multiple microservices into a central archive (e.g., ELK Stack).
2. **Data Pipeline ETL** — Combining CSVs from regional servers into a master dataset.
3. **Build Systems** — Concatenating source code modules before compilation/linking.
4. **Backup Utilities** — Consolidating incremental backups into a single archive.
5. **Compiler Design** — Merging symbol tables from multiple translation units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** In **Compiler Construction**, the symbol table from `lexical_analyzer.c` and `parser.c` are often merged by an `assembler` or `linker` into a single executable file — conceptually identical to merging two text files.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm Derivation — The "Standard KTU Board Solution"

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** of the merging algorithm, presented in a KTU board-friendly format. Every line is explained, with no implicit jumps.

### Algorithm (Pseudocode Form)

```
Step 1: START
Step 2: Define source file paths -> file1, file2
Step 3: Define destination file path -> file3
Step 4: OPEN file1 in READ mode ('r')
Step 5: READ all content of file1 -> data1
Step 6: CLOSE file1
Step 7: OPEN file2 in READ mode ('r')
Step 8: READ all content of file2 -> data2
Step 9: CLOSE file2
Step 10: OPEN file3 in WRITE mode ('w')
Step 11: WRITE data1 + data2 into file3
Step 12: CLOSE file3
Step 13: Print success message
Step 14: STOP
```

### Step-by-Step Python Code with Exhaustive Inline Commentary

```python
# ============================================================
# Program: Merge contents of two text files into a third file
# Course : PCCAL308 - Python Programming Lab
# Scheme : KTU 2024
# Module : 2 (File Handling)
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Define the file paths as string constants
# (Using relative paths for portability within the lab environment)
file1 = "file1.txt"   # Path to the first source text file
file2 = "file2.txt"   # Path to the second source text file
file3 = "merged.txt"  # Path to the destination (merged) text file

# Step 2: Read content from the first file
# The 'with' statement ensures the file is closed automatically
# even if an exception occurs (e.g., FileNotFoundError).
with open(file1, 'r') as f1:
    data1 = f1.read()  # Reads entire content as a single string

# Step 3: Read content from the second file (same logic as Step 2)
with open(file2, 'r') as f2:
    data2 = f2.read()  # Reads entire content as a single string

# Step 4: Merge (concatenate) the two strings
# Python's '+' operator concatenates strings left-to-right.
# data1's content appears FIRST, followed by data2's content.
merged_data = data1 + data2

# Step 5: Write the merged content to the third file
# Mode 'w' creates the file if it doesn't exist,
# or OVERWRITES it if it already exists.
with open(file3, 'w') as f3:
    f3.write(merged_data)

# Step 6: Confirmation message to the user
print(f"Files '{file1}' and '{file2}' have been merged into '{file3}' successfully.")
```

## 3.2 Derivation of Alternative Variants

### Variant A: Preserving a Newline Between the Two Contents

If `file1.txt` does not end with a newline, the contents of `file2.txt` will be **glued directly** to the last character of `file1.txt`. To prevent this, we add a newline delimiter:

$$
\text{merged\_data} = \text{data1} + \text{"\textbackslash n"} + \text{data2}
$$

```python
with open(file1, 'r') as f1:
    data1 = f1.read()

with open(file2, 'r') as f2:
    data2 = f2.read()

# Concatenation with explicit newline separator
merged_data = data1 + "\n" + data2

with open(file3, 'w') as f3:
    f3.write(merged_data)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If the candidate's `file1.txt` ends *without* a trailing `\n`, then `file2.txt`'s first line will be appended to `file1.txt`'s last line, creating a **garbled merged output** (e.g., "HelloWorld"). Examiners *do* deduct marks for this. Always insert `"\n"` explicitly when in doubt.

### Variant B: Line-by-Line Merge (Using `readlines`)

For better control over line ordering and to handle very large files efficiently, we can use `readlines()`:

```python
with open(file1, 'r') as f1, open(file2, 'r') as f2, open(file3, 'w') as f3:
    # Read all lines from each file into a list
    lines1 = f1.readlines()
    lines2 = f2.readlines()

    # Write line-by-line
    f3.writelines(lines1)
    f3.writelines(lines2)
```

### Variant C: Zip-Based Interleaved Merge (Alternate Alternation)

To **interleave** lines from both files (line 1 from file A, line 1 from file B, line 2 from A, line 2 from B, ...):

```python
with open(file1, 'r') as f1, open(file2, 'r') as f2, open(file3, 'w') as f3:
    for line1, line2 in zip(f1, f2):
        f3.write(line1)
        f3.write(line2)
```

> [!NOTE]
> Variant C is **not** the standard "concatenate" merge the KTU syllabus asks for, but it is a high-value **Part B (14-mark)** extension question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Approaches

| Criterion | `read()` + Concatenate | `readlines()` + `writelines()` | `zip()` Interleave |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory Efficiency | ❌ Loads entire file in RAM | ❌ Loads entire file in RAM | ✅ Stream-based (low RAM) |
| Code Simplicity | ✅ Very simple | ✅ Simple | ❌ Complex |
| Suitable for Huge Files | ❌ Not recommended | ❌ Not recommended | ✅ Recommended |
| Preserves Newlines | ⚠️ Only if source has them | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| KTU Standard Answer | ✅ Most expected | ✅ Acceptable | ❌ Rarely expected |
| Time Complexity | $O(n+m)$ | $O(n+m)$ | $O(\max(n,m))$ |

Where $n$ is the number of characters in `file1.txt` and $m$ is the number of characters in `file2.txt$.

## 3.4 Sample Input / Output Trace

Assume the following file contents:

**`file1.txt`:**
```
Hello from File 1.
This is line two.
```

**`file2.txt`:**
```
Welcome to File 2.
End of content.
```

**`merged.txt` (after running the program):**
```
Hello from File 1.
This is line two.Welcome to File 2.
End of content.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Note the missing newline** between "two." and "Welcome" in the above trace. This is because `file1.txt` lacks a trailing newline. Using **Variant A** (with explicit `"\n"`) fixes this.

**`merged.txt` (using Variant A):**
```
Hello from File 1.
This is line two.
Welcome to File 2.
End of content.
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Program Flowchart — File Merging Operation

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the control flow of the file merging program, from initialization to final file closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Define file paths: file1, file2, file3]
    B --> C[Open file1 in read mode r]
    C --> D{File1 exists?}
    D -- No --> E[Raise FileNotFoundError]
    E --> Z([END with Error])
    D -- Yes --> F[Read all content of file1 into data1]
    F --> G[Close file1 automatically via with]
    G --> H[Open file2 in read mode r]
    H --> I{File2 exists?}
    I -- No --> J[Raise FileNotFoundError]
    J --> Z
    I -- Yes --> K[Read all content of file2 into data2]
    K --> L[Close file2 automatically via with]
    L --> M[Concatenate: merged_data = data1 plus data2]
    M --> N[Open file3 in write mode w]
    N --> O[Write merged_data into file3]
    O --> P[Close file3 automatically via with]
    P --> Q[Print success message]
    Q --> R([END Success])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Merge Pipeline

The following block diagram shows the **high-level architecture** of how the two source files are processed by Python's I/O system and persisted into the destination file.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Layer Disk Storage]
        A1[file1.txt on Disk]
        A2[file2.txt on Disk]
    end

    subgraph PY[Python I/O Engine]
        B1[open file1 r]
        B2[read data1]
        B3[open file2 r]
        B4[read data2]
        B5[String Concatenation plus Operator]
        B6[open file3 w]
        B7[write merged data]
    end

    subgraph DEST[Destination Layer Disk Storage]
        C1[merged.txt on Disk]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3 --> B4
    B2 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> C1
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — File Pointer States

This diagram shows the lifecycle of the **file pointer** (the internal cursor that tracks the current read/write position) for each of the three files.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> OpenRead: open file1 r
    OpenRead --> Reading: read data1
    Reading --> AtEOF: pointer at byte n
    AtEOF --> Closed: with block exit auto close

    Closed --> OpenRead2: open file2 r
    OpenRead2 --> Reading2: read data2
    Reading2 --> AtEOF2: pointer at byte m
    AtEOF2 --> Closed2: with block exit auto close

    Closed2 --> OpenWrite: open file3 w
    OpenWrite --> Writing: write data1 plus data2
    Writing --> AtEOF3: pointer at byte n plus m
    AtEOF3 --> Closed3: with block exit auto close
    Closed3 --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Exception Handling Architecture (Best Practice Variant)

The following diagram depicts the **robust version** of the program with exception handling — a common KTU Part B extension.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[try block begins]
    B --> C[with open file1 r as f1]
    C --> D[with open file2 r as f2]
    D --> E[with open file3 w as f3]
    E --> F[f3.write f1.read plus f2.read]
    F --> G[print success]
    G --> H([END])
    C --> X1[except FileNotFoundError]
    D --> X1
    X1 --> Y[print file not found message]
    Y --> H
    E --> X2[except IOError]
    X2 --> Y
    F --> X3[except Exception as e]
    X3 --> Y
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In the exception handling diagram above, multiple `except` clauses are attached to the same `try` block conceptually. In actual Python code, they would be written as a chain: `except FileNotFoundError: ...` followed by `except IOError: ...` followed by `except Exception as e: ...`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between the file opening modes `'r'`, `'w'`, and `'a'` in Python? Give one use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `'r'` | Opens file in **read-only** mode. File must exist; otherwise `FileNotFoundError` is raised. Pointer starts at the **beginning**. | Reading source content for merging: `open("file1.txt", "r")` |
| `'w'` | Opens file in **write** mode. Creates a new file or **truncates** existing one. Pointer at the beginning. | Writing merged output: `open("merged.txt", "w")` |
| `'a'` | Opens file in **append** mode. Creates a new file or appends to existing. Pointer at the **end**. | Adding logs to a daily file: `open("app.log", "a")` |

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark per correct row with use case.)*

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the `with` statement preferred over manually calling `open()` and `close()` in Python file handling?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. The `with` statement is a **context manager** that **automatically closes** the file once the block is exited — even if an exception is raised inside the block. *(1 mark)*
2. It eliminates the need for explicit `f.close()`, thereby preventing **resource leaks** and **data corruption** due to unflushed buffers. *(1 mark)*
3. It makes the code **cleaner, more Pythonic, and exception-safe**, which is a recommended best practice in the KTU 2024 syllabus. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** A Part B question (14 marks) is split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, we provide **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent internal choices.

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A(a) [7 Marks]:** Write a Python program to merge the contents of two text files `data1.txt` and `data2.txt` into a third file `result.txt`. Display an appropriate success message.

**A(b) [7 Marks]:** Modify the above program to ensure that:
- A newline is added between the contents of the two files.
- The program handles `FileNotFoundError` gracefully.

**Model Solution for A(a):**

```python
# Program to merge two text files
file1 = "data1.txt"
file2 = "data2.txt"
file3 = "result.txt"

with open(file1, 'r') as f1:
    data1 = f1.read()

with open(file2, 'r') as f2:
    data2 = f2.read()

merged_data = data1 + data2

with open(file3, 'w') as f3:
    f3.write(merged_data)

print(f"Files '{file1}' and '{file2}' merged into '{file3}' successfully.")
```

**Mark Distribution (A-a):**
- Correct file path definitions — **1 mark**
- Correct `open()` with mode `'r'` and `with` statement — **2 marks**
- Correct reading using `.read()` — **1 mark**
- Correct concatenation using `+` — **1 mark**
- Correct write using `.write()` with mode `'w'` — **1 mark**
- Success message — **1 mark**

**Model Solution for A(b):**

```python
# Modified program with newline separator and exception handling
file1 = "data1.txt"
file2 = "data2.txt"
file3 = "result.txt"

try:
    with open(file1, 'r') as f1:
        data1 = f1.read()
    with open(file2, 'r') as f2:
        data2 = f2.read()

    # Insert newline between the two contents
    merged_data = data1 + "\n" + data2

    with open(file3, 'w') as f3:
        f3.write(merged_data)

    print(f"Merge successful: '{file3}' created.")

except FileNotFoundError as e:
    print(f"Error: One or more source files not found. Details: {e}")
```

**Mark Distribution (A-b):**
- Adding newline `"\\n"` between contents — **3 marks**
- Wrapping in `try-except` block — **2 marks**
- Correctly catching `FileNotFoundError` with informative message — **2 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B(a) [7 Marks]:** Write a Python program to count the total number of lines, words, and characters in a text file named `input.txt` and display the results.

**B(b) [7 Marks]:** Write a Python program to merge the line counts of two files `fileA.txt` and `fileB.txt` and write the line counts to a third file `count_summary.txt` in the format: `fileA: <count>` on line 1 and `fileB: <count>` on line 2.

**Model Solution for B(a):**

```python
# Program to count lines, words, and characters in a text file
filename = "input.txt"

with open(filename, 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()           # Returns a list of lines
    line_count = len(lines)         # Number of lines
    word_count = 0
    char_count = 0
    for line in lines:
        words = line.split()        # Splits line into words by whitespace
        word_count += len(words)
        char_count += len(line)     # Includes spaces and newline chars

print(f"Total lines      : {line_count}")
print(f"Total words      : {word_count}")
print(f"Total characters : {char_count}")
```

**Mark Distribution (B-a):**
- Correct file opening with `'r'` and `with` — **1 mark**
- Using `readlines()` and `len()` for line count — **1 mark**
- Iterating over lines and using `.split()` for words — **2 marks**
- Using `len(line)` for character count — **1 mark**
- Formatted output of all three counts — **2 marks**

**Model Solution for B(b):**

```python
# Program to merge line counts into a summary file
def count_lines(filename):
    with open(filename, 'r') as f:
        return len(f.readlines())

fileA = "fileA.txt"
fileB = "fileB.txt"
output = "count_summary.txt"

countA = count_lines(fileA)
countB = count_lines(fileB)

with open(output, 'w') as out:
    out.write(f"fileA: {countA}\n")
    out.write(f"fileB: {countB}\n")

print("Line count summary written successfully.")
```

**Mark Distribution (B-b):**
- Defining a function `count_lines()` with file open/read — **2 marks**
- Calling function for both files — **1 mark**
- Opening output file in write mode — **1 mark**
- Writing formatted output using f-strings with `\\n` — **2 marks**
- Success message — **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `with` statement and not closing files manually** → Up to **−2 marks** for resource-leak prone code.
> 2. **Using mode `'a'` instead of `'w'` for the destination file** → The merged content gets *appended* to an existing file instead of creating a fresh one. This is a **logic error** that costs **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Missing newline between the two merged contents** → Causes output concatenation like `"HelloWorld"`. Examiners test this with sample inputs that **lack** trailing newlines. Costs **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 4. **Hardcoding the file content inside the program (instead of reading from disk)** → Defeats the entire purpose of file handling. **−3 marks** (outright logical failure).
> 5. **Not handling `FileNotFoundError`** → In the 14-mark variant, missing `try-except` block costs **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing `read()` (returns `str`) with `readlines()` (returns `list`)** → Writing a list directly with `.write()` raises `TypeError`. Costs **−2 marks** in execution.
> 7. **Forgetting to include `encoding='utf-8'`** in `open()` (for non-ASCII content) → Causes `UnicodeDecodeError`. Minor deduction of **−1 mark** but recommended best practice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ File merging = **concatenation** of contents of two source files into a destination file.
- ✅ Standard pattern: `open(src, 'r')` → `.read()` → `open(dst, 'w')` → `.write()`.
- ✅ **Always** use the `with` statement — it is a context manager that **auto-closes** files and prevents resource leaks.
- ✅ File modes: `'r'` (read), `'w'` (write/truncate), `'a'` (append).
- ✅ Default mode is `'r'`. Default encoding is platform-dependent (use `encoding='utf-8'` for portability).
- ✅ `.read()` returns a single `str` containing the **entire file content**.
- ✅ `.readline()` returns **one line** as a `str`.
- ✅ `.readlines()` returns a **list of strings** (one per line, with `\n` preserved).
- ✅ `.write(s)` writes a string and returns the number of characters written.
- ✅ String concatenation in Python: `data1 + data2` (no automatic newline).
- ✅ To insert a separator: `data1 + "\n" + data2`.
- ✅ For very large files, use **streaming** techniques (loop over the file object line-by-line) to avoid loading everything into RAM.
- ✅ Exception handling: wrap file operations in `try-except FileNotFoundError` for robustness.
- ✅ KTU examiners expect the `with` statement as **mandatory** in the 14-mark variant.
- ✅ Binary files require modes like `'rb'` and `'wb'`, but the standard merge experiment uses **text** mode.
- ✅ The `os.path.exists(path)` function can be used as a **pre-check** before opening a file (alternative to try-except).
- ✅ Real-world analog: file merging is the foundation of **ETL pipelines, log aggregators, and build systems**.

---

topics.title: Write a Python program using NumPy to create an array of 10 random numbers and calculate the mean and standard deviation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL308 - PYTHON PROGRAMMING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Python program using NumPy to create an array of 10 random numbers and calculate the mean and standard deviation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**NumPy (Numerical Python)** is a fundamental open-source Python library used for scientific and numerical computing. It provides support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with a vast collection of high-level mathematical functions to operate on these arrays efficiently.

For the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCAL308 - Python Programming Lab**, this experiment requires the student to:

1. Create a one-dimensional NumPy array containing **10 random numbers**.
2. Compute the **Arithmetic Mean** of the array.
3. Compute the **Standard Deviation** of the array.
4. Display the generated array, the mean, and the standard deviation on the console.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - NumPy Fundamentals):**
> The experiment falls under the *NumPy* sub-module, which covers array creation (using `np.array()`, `np.zeros()`, `np.ones()`, `np.arange()`, and `np.random` functions), array slicing, broadcasting, and statistical operations (`mean`, `median`, `std`, `var`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **Class Teacher** who has just recorded the marks of **10 students** in a surprise test (each mark is a "random number" because you did not know what to expect). Once you have the marks, you naturally want to know two things:

- **"How did the class perform on average?"** → This is the **Mean** (the central typical value of the class).
- **"Were all students similar, or were some brilliant and some poor?"** → This is the **Standard Deviation** (a measure of *spread* — a small SD means the class was uniform, a large SD means the class had wide variation).

In this experiment, **NumPy acts like an automatic calculator** that generates 10 imaginary student marks, then instantly gives you both the average mark and the spread of the marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology at a Glance:**
> - **Random Number:** A number generated such that each value in a specified range has an equal probability of being selected.
> - **Mean ($\mu$ or $\bar{x}$):** The sum of all values divided by the count of values. It is the *center of mass* of the dataset.
> - **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$ or $s$):** The square root of the average of the squared deviations from the mean. It quantifies the *dispersion* or *spread* of the data around the mean.
> - **Default in NumPy:** `np.std()` uses the *population* formula (denominator = $N$, i.e., `ddof=0`).

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in Engineering

- **Data Science & Machine Learning:** The first step in *any* ML pipeline is to load a dataset and immediately compute summary statistics (mean, std, min, max). This experiment mirrors that exact workflow.
- **Signal Processing:** The standard deviation of noise in a signal is critical for calculating the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Engineers use $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to set **Six-Sigma** control limits on production lines.
- **Survey Analysis:** Statisticians use these metrics to summarize large-scale survey data in seconds using NumPy's vectorized operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Experiment

The program can be broken into **5 logical steps**, which is exactly how a KTU examiner expects you to explain it during the lab viva:

### Step 1 — Import the NumPy Library

The library is conventionally imported using the alias `np`. This is the universal standard in academia and industry (PEP 8 convention).

```python
import numpy as np
```

### Step 2 — Generate the Random Array

NumPy offers multiple random number generators under the `np.random` module. For this experiment, the three most common choices are:

| Function | Distribution | Range | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `np.random.rand(10)` | Uniform (continuous) | $[0.0, 1.0)$ | Default for floating-point experiments |
| `np.random.randint(low, high, size=10)` | Uniform (discrete integers) | $[\text{low}, \text{high})$ | Marks/age-style discrete data |
| `np.random.normal(loc, scale, size=10)` | Normal (Gaussian) | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Real-world noise simulation |

### Step 3 — Calculate the Mean

The function `np.mean()` (or its alias `array.mean()`) returns the arithmetic mean by summing all elements and dividing by $N$.

### Step 4 — Calculate the Standard Deviation

The function `np.std()` returns the standard deviation. By default, NumPy uses the *population* formula. To use the *sample* formula, set `ddof=1` (Delta Degrees of Freedom).

### Step 5 — Display the Results

Formatted printing using `print()` ensures a clean, professional output that the examiner can instantly grade.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete mathematical reference** for this experiment. **Memorize these formulas** — they are guaranteed to appear in the Part A viva questions and often in Part B derivations.

| Symbol | Formula Name | LaTeX Equation | NumPy Function | Denominator (Normalizer) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\bar{x}$ | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | `np.mean(arr)` | $N$ |
| $\sigma^2$ | Population Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | `np.var(arr)` | $N$ |
| $\sigma$ | Population Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | `np.std(arr)` | $N$ |
| $s^{2}$ | Sample Variance (unbiased) | $s^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | `np.var(arr, ddof=1)` | $N-1$ |
| $s$ | Sample Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | `np.std(arr, ddof=1)` | $N-1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Formulas:**
> - **Mean** → Used to compute the *expected value* in probability theory and the DC offset in electrical signals.
> - **Variance** → Used in the Wiener-Khinchin theorem (power spectral density) and in the formula for the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** in ML.
> - **Standard Deviation** → Used in Z-score normalization: $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, which is critical in feature scaling for neural networks.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Population vs. Sample

- **Population SD ($\sigma$):** Used when your array *is* the entire population (e.g., the marks of *all 10 students in your class*).
- **Sample SD ($s$):** Used when your array is a *sample* drawn from a larger population (e.g., you surveyed 10 citizens, but there are 10 million in your state). The denominator $N-1$ (Bessel's correction) provides an *unbiased* estimate.

In this KTU experiment with $N = 10$, NumPy's default `np.std()` returns the population SD. Always clarify this in your viva answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Manual Calculation (Step-by-Step Derivation)

To make the concept crystal clear, let us manually compute $\bar{x}$ and $\sigma$ for a small sample array before coding it.

**Sample Array** (assume `np.random.rand(10)` produced these 10 floating-point values):

$$x = [2.0,\ 4.0,\ 4.0,\ 4.0,\ 5.0,\ 5.0,\ 7.0,\ 9.0,\ 9.0,\ 9.0]$$

Here, $N = 10$.

### Derivation 1 — Computing the Mean ($\bar{x}$)

We apply the formula: $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i &= 2 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 6 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 10 + 5 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 15 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 20 + 7 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 27 + 9 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 36 + 9 + 9 \\
&= 45 + 9 \\
&= 54
\end{aligned}
$$

Now divide by $N = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{54}{10} = 5.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The mean of the array is **$\bar{x} = 5.4$**.

### Derivation 2 — Computing the Population Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)

We apply the formula: $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$.

**Step 2.1:** Compute the deviation of each element from the mean $(x_i - \bar{x})$:

| Index $i$ | $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2.0 | $2.0 - 5.4 = -3.4$ |
| 2 | 4.0 | $4.0 - 5.4 = -1.4$ |
| 3 | 4.0 | $4.0 - 5.4 = -1.4$ |
| 4 | 4.0 | $4.0 - 5.4 = -1.4$ |
| 5 | 5.0 | $5.0 - 5.4 = -0.4$ |
| 6 | 5.0 | $5.0 - 5.4 = -0.4$ |
| 7 | 7.0 | $7.0 - 5.4 = 1.6$ |
| 8 | 9.0 | $9.0 - 5.4 = 3.6$ |
| 9 | 9.0 | $9.0 - 5.4 = 3.6$ |
| 10 | 9.0 | $9.0 - 5.4 = 3.6$ |

**Step 2.2:** Square each deviation to get $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$:

| Index $i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $-3.4$ | $11.56$ |
| 2 | $-1.4$ | $1.96$ |
| 3 | $-1.4$ | $1.96$ |
| 4 | $-1.4$ | $1.96$ |
| 5 | $-0.4$ | $0.16$ |
| 6 | $-0.4$ | $0.16$ |
| 7 | $1.6$ | $2.56$ |
| 8 | $3.6$ | $12.96$ |
| 9 | $3.6$ | $12.96$ |
| 10 | $3.6$ | $12.96$ |

**Step 2.3:** Sum all the squared deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} &= 11.56 + 1.96 + 1.96 + 1.96 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 13.52 + 1.96 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 15.48 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 15.64 + 0.16 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 15.80 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 18.36 + 12.96 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 31.32 + 12.96 + 12.96 \\
&= 44.28 + 12.96 \\
&= 57.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.4:** Divide by $N$ to get the variance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{2} &= \frac{57.24}{10} = 5.724
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.5:** Take the square root to get the standard deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{5.724} \approx 2.3925
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The population standard deviation is **$\sigma \approx 2.3925$**.

## 3.2 Complete Python Program (Model Answer for KTU Record)

```python
# Program: Generate 10 random numbers using NumPy and calculate mean & standard deviation
# Course  : PCCAL308 - Python Programming Lab
# Module  : 2 (NumPy Fundamentals)

import numpy as np

# Step 1: Create an array of 10 random numbers in the range [0.0, 1.0)
random_array = np.random.rand(10)

# Step 2: Calculate the arithmetic mean
mean_value = np.mean(random_array)

# Step 3: Calculate the population standard deviation
std_value = np.std(random_array)

# Step 4: Display the results
print("Generated Random Array :", random_array)
print("Arithmetic Mean (mu)   :", mean_value)
print("Standard Deviation (sd):", std_value)
```

### Sample Output

```
Generated Random Array : [0.5488135  0.71518937 0.60276338 0.54488318 0.4236548
 0.64589411 0.43758721 0.891773   0.96366276 0.38344152]
Arithmetic Mean (mu)   : 0.6157662838624956
Standard Deviation (sd): 0.1883462207439568
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the output changes every time:** `np.random.rand(10)` draws fresh samples from NumPy's global Mersenne Twister PRNG (Pseudo-Random Number Generator). For **reproducible** output — which is often required in lab records — use `np.random.seed(42)` *before* generating the array. The value `42` is the famous "Answer to the Ultimate Question" from *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy*.

### Reproducible Variant (Bonus for Record)

```python
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)                       # Fixes the random sequence
random_array = np.random.rand(10)
mean_value  = np.mean(random_array)
std_value   = np.std(random_array)

print("Random Array  :", random_array)
print("Mean          :", mean_value)
print("Std Deviation :", std_value)
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: NumPy Functions at a Glance

| NumPy Function | Purpose | Equivalent Manual Math | Returns |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `np.random.rand(10)` | Generates 10 uniform random floats | $x_i \sim U(0, 1)$ | ndarray of shape $(10,)$ |
| `np.mean(arr)` | Computes $\bar{x}$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ | scalar float |
| `np.std(arr)` | Computes $\sigma$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | scalar float |
| `np.var(arr)` | Computes $\sigma^2$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | scalar float |
| `np.min(arr)` / `np.max(arr)` | Range of the array | $\min(x_i)$, $\max(x_i)$ | scalar float |
| `arr.shape` | Dimensions of the array | N/A | tuple |
| `arr.size` | Total number of elements | $N$ | integer |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Program Flow Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **exact execution sequence** of the Python program, which is a frequently asked viva question.

```mermaid

    I --> J[Print mean_value using print]
    J --> K[Print std_value using print]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NumPy Operations

The following diagram maps the **internal functional architecture** of how NumPy executes this experiment, showing the relationship between the Python interpreter, the NumPy C-backend, and the Mersenne Twister PRNG.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Python Script] --> Q[NumPy API Layer]
    Q --> R[Mersenne Twister PRNG]
    Q --> S[Vectorized C Backend]
    R --> T[Random Array of 10 floats]

    S --> U[Mean Calculation Engine]
    S --> V[Variance Engine]
    S --> W[Square Root Engine]

    W --> Y[Final Output std value]

```

## 4.3 Visual Concept Map — Mean vs Standard Deviation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[10 Random Numbers x1 to xN] --> B[Arithmetic Mean]
    A --> C[Deviation from Mean]
    B --> D[Center of Data]
```
