# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Scheme**: Each Part-A question carries **3 marks**, with **1 mark for keyword accuracy**, **1 mark for clarity**, and **1 mark for example/syntax**.

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: List any three keyword arguments of `matplotlib.pyplot.plot()` that control the visual appearance of a line graph. Give a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
1. **`marker`**: Specifies the symbol used for each data point (e.g., `'o'` for circle, `'s'` for square). *(1 mark)*
2. **`linestyle`**: Controls the pattern of the connecting line (e.g., `'-'` solid, `'--'` dashed, `':'` dotted). *(1 mark)*
3. **`color`**: Sets the hue of the line and markers, accepted as named strings (`'red'`) or hex codes (`'#FF0000'`). *(1 mark)*

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: What is the role of the `plt.show()` function in a Matplotlib script? Why must it be the last plotting statement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- The `plt.show()` function **triggers the rendering pipeline** and **displays the figure** on the active GUI backend (e.g., TkAgg window). *(1 mark)*
- It **starts the GUI event loop**, which is **blocking** — meaning the Python interpreter pauses at this line until the user closes the figure window. *(1 mark)*
- It is placed at the end because any plotting or annotation call **after** `plt.show()` is applied to a new figure (or is ignored), making the plot appear incomplete. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Each Part-B question has **internal choice** — students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Part B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Write a Python program using Matplotlib to plot a line graph for the following monthly sales data of a bookstore:**

| Month | Sales (₹) |
|---|---|
| Jan | 12000 |
| Feb | 15000 |
| Mar | 18000 |
| Apr | 16000 |
| May | 20000 |
| Jun | 22000 |

**Add a title, axis labels, markers, grid, and a legend. Save the figure as 'bookstore_sales.png' with 300 dpi.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Program Code

**Model Solution**:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# (a) Data preparation (1 mark)
months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun']
sales  = [12000, 15000, 18000, 16000, 20000, 22000]

# (b) Figure and plot (2 marks)
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(months, sales,
         marker='D',                    # diamond marker
         linestyle='-',                 # solid line
         color='blue',
         linewidth=2,
         markersize=10,
         label='Bookstore Sales 2024')

# (c) Annotations (2 marks)
plt.title('Monthly Sales - Bookstore 2024', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Month', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Sales (in ₹)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')

# (d) Save and show (2 marks)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('bookstore_sales.png', dpi=300)
plt.show()
```

**Mark Distribution**:
- Correct import & data: **1 mark**
- `plt.plot()` with style kwargs: **2 marks**
- Title, xlabel, ylabel: **1.5 marks**
- Grid + legend: **1.5 marks**
- `savefig` with 300 dpi + `show`: **1 mark**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Conceptual Follow-up

**Q: Explain why `dpi=300` is used and what happens if `plt.savefig()` is called *after* `plt.show()`.**

**Model Solution (7 marks)**:

- **`dpi=300`**: Dots-Per-Inch defines the **resolution** of the exported image. A **300 dpi** PNG is the **print-quality standard** — sharp on A4 paper. *(2 marks)*
- The default `dpi=100` produces a low-resolution, **screen-only** image unsuitable for printed lab records. *(1 mark)*
- **`plt.show()` triggers a blocking GUI event loop**. If `savefig` is called *after* `show()` in a script, the figure object is **already finalized** but in some backends the **canvas is cleared upon window close**, leading to a **blank or zero-byte PNG**. *(2 marks)*
- **Best practice**: Always call `plt.savefig()` **before** `plt.show()` so the file is written while the figure object is still in memory and active. *(2 marks)*

---

### Part B — Choice B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: A software company wants to compare its monthly revenue for the years 2023 and 2024. Write a Python program using Matplotlib to plot both years as two line graphs on the *same* figure, with appropriate legend, title, and styling. The data is given below:**

| Month | 2023 Revenue (₹) | 2024 Revenue (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 50000 | 55000 |
| Feb | 52000 | 58000 |
| Mar | 48000 | 60000 |
| Apr | 53000 | 62000 |
| May | 55000 | 65000 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Program Code

**Model Solution**:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# (a) Data (1 mark)
months    = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May']
revenue_23 = [50000, 52000, 48000, 53000, 55000]
revenue_24 = [55000, 58000, 60000, 62000, 65000]

# (b) Two plot() calls on same axes (2 marks)
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))

plt.plot(months, revenue_23,
         marker='o', linestyle='-', color='red',
         linewidth=2, markersize=8,
         label='2023')

plt.plot(months, revenue_24,
         marker='s', linestyle='--', color='blue',
         linewidth=2, markersize=8,
         label='2024')

# (c) Annotations (2 marks)
plt.title('Monthly Revenue Comparison: 2023 vs 2024',
          fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Month', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Revenue (in ₹)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.7)
plt.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=11)

# (d) Layout and render (2 marks)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Mark Distribution**:
- Data + 2 `plot()` calls: **3 marks**
- Distinct color/marker/linestyle per series: **1.5 marks**
- Title, xlabel, ylabel, grid, legend: **2 marks**
- `tight_layout` and `show`: **0.5 marks**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Conceptual Follow-up

**Q: What is the purpose of the `label` parameter in each `plot()` call? What would happen if it were omitted?**

**Model Solution (7 marks)**:

- The `label` parameter provides the **human-readable name** for that data series, which is consumed by the **`plt.legend()`** function to build the color-to-series key. *(2 marks)*
- If the `label` is omitted, `plt.legend()` would have **no entries** to display, resulting in either an **empty legend box** (with a `UserWarning: No handles with labels found`) or **no legend at all** depending on the Matplotlib version. *(2 marks)*
- **Best practice**: Always set a unique `label` for every series in a multi-line plot. The labels should be **descriptive** (e.g., `'2023 Q1'`) rather than generic (e.g., `'Series 1'`). *(1 mark)*
- **Bonus**: A passing student may also mention that `label` is *lazy* — it is **not rendered** until `plt.legend()` is explicitly called. *(2 marks)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCCAL308 Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`** → -2 marks (program won't run).
> 2. **Mismatched list lengths** (e.g., 11 months with 12 sales values) → `ValueError` at runtime → 0 marks for output.
> 3. **Missing `plt.title` / `plt.xlabel` / `plt.ylabel`** → -1.5 marks cumulatively. These are **non-negotiable** for a passing plot.
> 4. **No grid lines** → -0.5 to -1 mark. The examiner checks for `plt.grid(True)`.
> 5. **Calling `plt.show()` before all annotations** → Annotations appear on a *new* empty figure. Result: empty plot window. → -3 marks.
> 6. **Confusing `plt.plot()` with `plt.scatter()`** → The former connects points with a line; the latter does not. Using scatter for a line-graph question is **conceptually wrong** → -2 marks.
> 7. **Not saving the figure** when explicitly asked → -1 mark.
> 8. **Hardcoding 12 months but providing only 6 sales values** → Crashes. Always cross-verify list lengths.
> 9. **Using `plt.legend()` without `label=`** → Empty legend, examiner docks marks.
> 10. **Viva trap**: Examiner asks *"Why is `plt` the standard alias?"* — Answer: **PEP 8 convention + community standard; minimizes typing and improves readability.**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCCAL308 — Experiment 2**

- **Library**: `matplotlib.pyplot` is imported as `plt`. *(PEP 8 standard)*
- **Core call**: `plt.plot(x, y)` is the **fundamental** line-graph function.
- **Data rule**: `len(x)` **MUST EQUAL** `len(y)`. Mismatch → `ValueError`.
- **Mandatory annotations**: `plt.title()`, `plt.xlabel()`, `plt.ylabel()` — **never omit**.
- **Grid**: `plt.grid(True)` with optional `linestyle` and `alpha` arguments.
- **Styling kwargs** of `plot()`: `marker`, `linestyle`, `color`, `linewidth`, `markersize`, `label`.
- **Marker codes**: `'o'` (circle), `'s'` (square), `'^'` (triangle up), `'D'` (diamond), `'*'` (star).
- **Line styles**: `'-'` (solid), `'--'` (dashed), `'-.''` (dash-dot), `':'` (dotted).
- **Color codes**: `'r'` (red), `'g'` (green), `'b'` (blue), `'k'` (black), `'m'` (magenta), `'c'` (cyan). Hex codes like `'#FF5733'` also accepted.
- **Figure size**: `plt.figure(figsize=(w, h))` — w and h are in **inches**. Recommended 10×6 or 8×5 for monthly data.
- **Save**: `plt.savefig('filename.png', dpi=300)` — call **before** `plt.show()` for guaranteed export.
- **Show**: `plt.show()` is the **last statement**; it blocks until the window is closed.
- **Layout polish**: `plt.tight_layout()` prevents label/title cropping.
- **Multi-series**: Multiple `plt.plot()` calls on the same axes; each must have a **unique** `label`.
- **Legend**: Requires `label` in `plot()`; displayed via `plt.legend(loc=...)`.
- **X-tick rotation**: `plt.xticks(rotation=45)` prevents overlap for long month names.
- **Categorical X-axis**: Strings (month names) are auto-mapped to integer indices `[0..N-1]`.
- **Default backend**: `Agg` (non-interactive) or `TkAgg` (interactive pop-up) depending on environment.
- **Common error**: `ModuleNotFoundError` → fix with `pip install matplotlib`.
- **Viva gold line**: *"Matplotlib was created by John D. Hunter in 2003 and is the most widely used Python visualization library, forming the basis for higher-level libraries like Seaborn and Pandas plotting."*
- **One-line definition to memorize**: *"A line graph is a plot of ordered pairs connected by line segments, used to visualize trends in continuous or discrete time-series data."*
